en_body:
The Will — Reluctantly Lifting the Flower
 A sound falls—
 and vanishes
 Nothing remains at all
 in the Existence that once was
Day and night, I and they, that Life—
 unforgiving, yet how very Vast!
 Poetry is wind
 Wind stirs the storm
 the heart's storm is a piece of dream
 To bite into dream
 is to stand leaning at the threshold
 of endless, alien dreaming
Between the storm and poetry
the borders fold upon themselves
I humbly lean upon the staff of Heaven-and-Earth
down the muddy footpath
I beg the flowers' forgiveness—
 after the storm
 flowers always fall
 their bodies feigning...

(28/12/2006)
note: The title particle 'à' is a Vietnamese sighing exclamation (akin to a soft 'ah') held as-is; 'gượng' means doing something against one's failing strength or will, distinct from mere reluctance — closer to forcing oneself to a gentle act one can barely perform.
issues: Line 6 — 'không bao-dung, bao-la lắm sao!' contains untranslatable wordplay: 'bao' appears in both 'bao-dung' (tolerance/forgiveness) and 'bao-la' (vast/immense), the shared syllable enacting the paradox. 'Unforgiving, yet how very Vast!' preserves the contrast but loses the sonic echo.

Line 9 — 'miếng mộng': 'miếng' (a piece/morsel/bite) deliberately sets up the eating metaphor in line 10 ('cắn vào' = bite into). 'A piece of dream' is cleaner but 'a mouthful of dream' is more faithful to the food-register.

Lines 23-24 — 'xác vờ...' (body feigning/pretending): the implied object — feigning death — is left fully silent by the ellipsis in the original. Both translators render this correctly; the suspension is the meaning.

en_body:
HOÀNG-ÂN
Half-Moon of Love's Season (1)


 A grain of sand, I drift downstream along Space-Time; the glass hourglass cinched at its waist and caressed smooth — at the midpoint of a luminous human life of sutras and scripture: the Selves imprisoned in the realm of No-Self: Oh, how poetic, that ancient Vietnamese word — when naming only oneself, it reaches not for "tôi" [I] but for "chúng tôi" [we], as in the epilogues of scripture and old tales.

 ...Suddenly musing like Zhuangzi, blurred between man and butterfly dreaming each other: who dreamt into being "my dream"? What suppressed longing created the first eruption of the science-mythology Big Bang...drifting into the stillness of theorems and laws, of bodies journeying far through layer upon layer of Universe? But still: the orbits of the cosmos are the society of humankind — orbits tangled endlessly by bombs and bullets into poverty: who first erected the concept of Power to breed Injustice?

 ...Mechanical Civilization has produced physical masses — like radio, like television, like the box-molds of high-rise buildings, like iron eagles whose bellies carry bombs that know how to seek their own targets — has made concrete even Faith itself! Blood and flesh remain red and soft before pain, though sophisticated bombs and bullets have replaced spears, swords, bows and arrows. Amid the tragic deaths of war, even the comparison to the suffering of God's own Son Giê-Su upon the cross suddenly loses all the value of its bestowal! Since Darwinism rose up to burn the scripture "Genesis" and replaced it with Kant's pronouncement: "God is dead!" — Humanity labors to find "the order of chaos"!

 Can it be simplified, when the Adams are merely Neanderthal men? And whether Mary or Eve, the Creator always needs a Woman to hold and carry the karma of all humanity — be it Giê-Su, the Buddhas, or the saints.
note: Hoàng-Ân braids Zhuangzi's butterfly-dream (Daoist) with Buddhist No-Self and the Christian cross, then pivots to Darwinism and Kant — her syncretic method is the poem's argument, not its ornament.
issues: "lời bạt" = postscript/epilogue (correctly rendered as "epilogues"; Version B's "prefaces" is an error — fixed here)
"Ẩn-ức" carries a psychoanalytic charge (repressed/suppressed desire) that "suppressed longing" softens — no cleaner English equivalent
"nhân-quả" = cause-and-effect/karma; "karma" chosen as more immediate but elides the Buddhist causality pairing
"thần-thoại khoa-học" compound kept as "science-mythology" (closer to the hyphenated original) rather than the adjectival "scientific-mythological"
The "God is dead" pronouncement is attributed to Kant in the original — faithfully reproduced as the poet wrote it, though the phrase is Nietzsche's

en_title: BÙI NGỌC TẤN: STORY TOLD IN THE YEAR 2000
en_body:
BÙI NGỌC TẤN: STORY TOLD IN THE YEAR 2000
(02/02/2013)

 Bùi Ngọc Tấn's true name is Bùi Đức Tấn, born in 1934 in Hải-Phòng, North Vietnam. He first came to be known through his debut short story, "Chị Trúc" — which took first prize in a literary competition organized by the newspaper Văn-Nghệ: in "Chị Trúc," Bùi Ngọc Tấn described the losses of a citizen living under the communist society of the North. He was arrested and sent to re-education from 1968 to 1972. Released, he continued to pursue the path he had drawn for himself. He brought forth the memoir MỘT THỜI ĐỂ MẤT, and two short story collections: NHỮNG NGƯỜI RÁCH VIỆC, and MỘT NGÀY DÀI ĐẰNG-ĐẴNG. As noted in the afterword at the close of the work CHUYỆN KỂ NĂM 2000, Bùi Ngọc Tấn completed this near-thousand-page work at Ngã Sáu Sài-Gòn from June 1990 through November 1991. CHUYỆN KỂ NĂM 2000 was issued by the domestic publisher Thanh-Niên in February 2000, and was immediately — under Decision No. 395/QĐ-BVHTT of the Vietnamese Communist Ministry of Information and Culture — suspended from distribution and recalled for destruction.

 CHUYỆN KỂ NĂM 2000 ought to be read as a novelized autobiography. It is not quite a memoir in the manner of ĐÊM GIỮA BAN NGÀY: HỒI-KÝ CỦA MỘT NGƯỜI KHÔNG LÀM CHÍNH-TRỊ by Vũ Thư Hiên; nor a work of pure invention like WAR AND PEACE by Leo Tolstoy. The details belonging to the protagonist of CHUYỆN KỂ NĂM 2000 — "Hắn," He — are freighted by Bùi Ngọc Tấn with many particulars that coincide with the author himself: also born in 1934; also a writer; also imprisoned for five years of re-education because he, too, dared to speak the Truth entirely unfavorable to the Vietnamese communist regime — that is, to the international communist regime entire!

 Allow me to cite a few passages from CHUYỆN KỂ NĂM HAI NGÀN by Bùi Ngọc Tấn for us to savor together.

 MỘT KHOẢNG TRỜI XUÂN: "That year, too, brought the terrible relentless rain. A mist-rain — that finest of rains,
 whitening the forests, whitening the mountains, adrift and billowing, swaying back to the
 prehistoric. Only the tiniest dust-specks flying and turning in the air, yet all of it
 drenched through. To touch anything was to shudder with the wet. His canvas forest-
 shoes, left unattended three days, had already grown mushrooms. That rain which whitened
 the trẩu-tree hills along the road they walked — from camp to worksite and back again. The
 trẩu trees, their young tender leaves radiation-soft with spring, in just one night burst
 all at once into boundless clusters of small pinkish buds...He walked to labor beside his
 fellow prisoners — exhausted, heavy-hearted, and without a single thought of the day of
 return. They were prisoners without sentences. They went to concentrated re-education."

 MÁU TRONG RỪNG ỚT: "One afternoon returning from hoeing cassava at a newly-burned field, the guard officer,
 the armed officer — suddenly saw the prisoners all running into the forest. Officer Vui,
 the guard in charge of the work team, cried out: 'What is going on?!' The armed officer
 bellowed: 'Stand still! A revolt?!!!' No one listened. The gray-clad figures ran to the
 forest's edge. He lunged after his comrades.
 Warning shots split the eardrums. No matter! A forest of sky-pointing chili peppers thrusting
 their blood-red fruits heavenward. Not even in dreams would you see such a thing. Across
 a stream. Climbing a stretch entirely of săng grass and low-crouching saplings. Reaching
 the old-growth forest. A perennial old-growth chili grove, vast and abundantly lush...More shots
 fired into the sky. Nothing could make the prisoner comrades stop. All rushed forward
 as if possessed with Madness. Pluck. Pick."
note: \"Nhiễm-xạ\" (irradiated/suffused-with-radiation) applied to spring-lit young leaves is Bùi Ngọc Tấn's own unusual scientific metaphor; \"radiation-soft\" preserves his deliberate strangeness rather than smoothing it to \"suffused with the radiance of spring.\"
issues: "trẩu" tree (tung/wood-oil tree) kept as transliterated proper name rather than botanical equivalent — preserves Vietnamese specificity but may obscure the image for English readers

"nhiễm-xạ mùa xuân" — rendered as "radiation-soft with spring," preserving the author's unusual scientific metaphor (nhiễm-xạ = irradiated/saturated); this is faithful but strange, which is precisely the author's intent

"Họ đi tập-trung cải-tạo" — "They went to concentrated re-education" retains the regime's own stilted euphemism rather than rendering it as "concentration camp"; the awkwardness is intentional and faithful

"bọn hắn" (his group/bunch, colloquial) is dissolved into "they" in the trẩu-hill sentence — a minor loss of the original's slightly contemptuous register toward the prisoner group as seen from outside
Closing quotation mark on MÁU TRONG RỪNG ỚT passage: absent in the Vietnamese source file; added here by inference from context

en_body:
the moon — thick as condensed milk —
pours straight down upon my crown
so I see only
moonlight, not the moon;

the circumference, bewildered, asks
how this white Mystery can be,
the surface area lies spread in milk,
questioning — unanswered —
in the zone
where Space breathes
and invites Time to sit and play

(UH, central campus, houston, tx
university center, first floor:
dear friend and swiss chocolate &
the beatles' White Album)
note: Chân-như is the Vietnamese rendering of the Buddhist Sanskrit tathatā (真如 in Chinese), meaning the bare fact of things as they are — Suchness or Thusness — the poem enacts it by dissolving the observer into the observed (moonlight without the moon).
issues: Line 6: 'làm sao' is multivalent — 'how this white Mystery can be' captures existential bewilderment but 'làm sao' could lean toward 'in what manner' rather than ontological 'can be'

Line 7: 'phơi sưa' is compact and unusual — 'lies spread in milk' reads phơi as spread-out and sưa as milk, but phơi also carries a sense of being aired/exposed under open sky; 'bathed in milk' is an alternative
Title: 'Suchness' is the standard English for chân-như (tathatā); 'Thusness' is the other accepted rendering — both are defensible

en_body:
"My heart is like a sunflower—
Of ten directions, it turns only toward the one direction of the Sun."*

Yellow flowers, yellow flowers, yellow flowers
Đà-Lạt, so full of Grace—
Golden flowers, golden flowers, golden flowers
A whole Heaven of Flowers, ravishingly Beautiful..!!

I dream of becoming a white butterfly,
Wandering through a land of flowers without end—
Where even the color of sunlight grows gentle,
And leaves the cheeks sweetly dreaming—
The Flowers that know how to speak, beautifully dreaming.

"It is told: once there was a man who left his post,
He climbed the mountain to find the grotto of yellow flowers, to sleep deep."**
Come, return here — be lulled into the Sleep of Forgetting,
A field of flowers, white butterflies drifting in dream...


* Ca-Dao Việt-Nam
** Thơ Phạm Thiên Thư, "Động Hoa Vàng"
note: Stanza 3's quoted couplet is from Phạm Thiên Thư's "Động Hoa Vàng" — a famous 1970s Vietnamese poem about a scholar who abandons his post to live among mountain flowers; the allusion frames Đà-Lạt as that same mythic retreat.
issues: "ngây ngây bờ má" — ngây ngây means dreamily dazed or sweetly stupefied (not quite dreaming); "sweetly dreaming" softens it toward the poem's atmosphere at slight cost to the word's bemused/dazed exactness.

"hữu-tình" carries romantic/sentimental charge ("full of feeling, full of sentiment") rather than religious Grace — "so full of Grace" imports a slight Christian resonance absent in the Vietnamese, though consistent with the poet's syncretic register.
"bờ má" = the cheeks' edge/surface; both versions flatten to "the cheeks" — minor loss of tactile delicacy.

en_title: Stone Has Always Been Without Knowing
en_body:
Stone Has Always Been Without Knowing
Đá Vốn Vô-Tri
(06/03/2007)

...The jade-green mountain stretches into miracle, a child gazes upon his reflection in search of himself: and learns to love that deep-saturated green, drenched in the mirroring of the silken sky with its drifting veils of cotton-white cloud. Jade-stone — cool against the small palm and the curve of his cheek when he presses his face into that back wall of sublime perfection.

...The months pass, and the small boy grows by degrees — busy with the many demanding yet delightful roles the world requires of him...yet the young man still returns to the stone mountain to gaze and find his reflection, and still presses his face against the back wall of jade stone.

...His father would sit in the side-room of the back garden, carving jewelry for his mother. He would often watch those intricate ornaments shimmer upon her, and tell himself: I am a happy person — for my parents love each other and are joyful together, beside me!

...Until one day his parents called him to them and gave the news: they had decided upon divorce — for his mother had "learned to love"...Happiness became suddenly a gray region of murky shadow: he lost his footing on the plank bridge with its loosened nails, the one called the life of human love!

...And the shadows of those tender cotton-white cloud clusters, shimmering and reflected upon the wall of the jade mountain — like a Betrayal. His father had stopped sitting in the back garden carving jewelry for his mother; and his mother had left behind the old jewelry her husband had once so carefully, lovingly carved for her.

...He is soon to leave — abandoning the mythical realm of the sky's silhouette dimly imprinted upon the back wall of the jade stone mountain, of the fairy-tale peak that crumbles now, no longer nourishing the rosy Imagination. He stills his carving hands and gazes at the jewelry box he carved to hold his mother's old pieces. He places them — and the poems he had written in tribute to his parents' ardent love — into the box, and closes the lid...After burying it beneath the roots of the peach tree beside the side-room where his father used to sit carving stone: he will leave this place and the sacred jade stone mountain behind — as Happiness has betrayed him!
note: The mother's phrase "biết yêu" (literally "came to know love / learned to love") is kept in quotation marks as in the Vietnamese — the quotes signal irony and euphemism for an extramarital affair, which neither translator footnotes but both correctly preserve through the scare-quotes.
issues: 'chái nhà' (the attached lean-to/side annex) — rendered as 'side-room' throughout; neither 'shed', 'lean-to', nor 'side-room' is exact, but 'side-room' is the least misleading

'vùng mầu xám che bóng mập-mờ' — literally 'a gray zone that covers with murky/dim shadow'; both versions compress this into 'gray region of murky shadow', which is defensible but loses the active 'covers/casts'
'dáng trời' = 'the sky's shape/form' — rendered as 'silhouette' which adds visual specificity not quite in the original

Title: 'vốn' carries the sense of 'originally by nature / has always been' — 'Has Always Been Without Knowing' captures the temporal permanence but loses the ontological weight of 'inherent/original nature'

en_title: The Lute Cries: Tích Tịch Tình Tang
en_body:
tình tang tính tính tang tình
whose Lute — each trembling string becoming sound —
tính tình tang tính tình tang
whose Heart — its waves rippling into echoing murmur —
tích tình tang tính tình tình
my Soul — how it softens, like clouds that slip from the sky —

you bow above the strings, drawing your very Self forth —
I hear the Soul turning on each degree of the scale

"đàn kêu tích tịch tình tang —
who carries the Princess up the ladder — home to Heaven?"

long ago who wove together this song —
I sit now, secretly moved to tears by whose music

how much Rapture you pour forth, how much you give —
I sit, powerless, sensing in silence your innermost thought

Bá-Nha must have his Tử-Kỳ —
how then can I speak the heart's depths for the listener to hear?

Tử-Kỳ without his Bá-Nha —
who then can awaken Soul-Recognition within the lute's cry?

Tang tình tang tính tình tang
Tình ơi, Tình hỡi:
To love Love — here it is!!

( for LuiKang of Nhac Death )

(18/10/2007)
note: Bá-Nha and Tử-Kỳ are the legendary Chinese musician-and-listener pair (伯牙/子期) whose story defines 'tri-âm' — true mutual recognition through music; when Tử-Kỳ died, Bá-Nha broke his lute, declaring no one left could ever truly hear him.
issues: Line 7 'người nghiêng trước phím gọi mình': 'người' is third-person ('the player'), but direct address ('you') follows B's interpretation for intimacy — not explicit in the Vietnamese.
Line 8 'hồn trở trên từng bậc-cung': 'trở' means to turn/return, not specifically ascend; 'turning on each degree' is literal but the directional sense (up the scale) remains somewhat uncertain.
Line 18 'tri-âm' (知音, Sino-Vietnamese): 'Soul-Recognition' is a reasonable gloss but loses the specifically musical dimension of the term — recognizing a kindred spirit through sound.
Lines 13–14 shift to 'you' for 'người' (the player) to maintain consistency with line 7's direct address; the Vietnamese keeps 'người' (third person) throughout.

en_body:
Light opens slowly through the gap in the curtain
The sky still in its predawn hush — Dawn not yet come
Morning dew trembling on the carpet of new grass
The birds' trembling voices, sparse — calling the day

I go out to visit my Hibiscus in its pot —
one bud still coiled tight as a sealed trumpet
I sprinkle water and pick off the worm-eaten leaves;
A cup of black coffee, strong and hot
and a morning cigarette
with National Public Radio
interviewing Howard Dean;
A report on the new Medicare law;
President Bush and the visit to England
with the anti-Iraq-war protests;
USS Vandercliff docked at the Saigon River pier
this morning: Vietnam renovating???

The sunlight has just settled
so lightly on my shoulders
trembling trembling as if wanting to laugh, yet hurried and bewildered
with the burning-hot news of the times!

I go into the bathroom, comb my hair, brush my teeth
change clothes, put on shoes
go to the bus stop
go to the library
...A day has just begun...

(2003)
note: Line 19 preserves 'trembling trembling' to mirror the Vietnamese reduplication 'run run', which is a deliberate rhythmic device the poet uses to slow the line and enact physical sensation — collapsing it to a single 'trembling' (as Version B does) loses that stutter.
issues: Line 16: 'Việt-Nam đổi mới???' — 'renovating' catches the literal irony (sprucing up for the warship visit) but loses the specific resonance of Đổi Mới as Vietnam's economic reform program; 'reforming' reads more political but is also defensible

Line 2: 'tinh-mơ' is imprecise in both versions — it means the pure, still quality of the very earliest morning, neither quite 'haze' nor quite 'hush'; 'predawn hush' chosen for sonic fit with her voice
Line 4: 'Giọng chim' is grammatically singular in Vietnamese (birdsong / bird-voice) but functions collectively; 'birds' trembling voices' is a reasonable plural rendering rather than a single bird

en_body:
(23/10/2007)

...the night: wind, rain: branches writhing and flailing outside...in the small room, the heater-flame murmurs low: suddenly Poetry returns after a long season of distant indifference...why? why? ...is it only when one can no longer flee the deep-aching Pain, and the fears that must face the Truth — a Truth that stamps the imprint of Solitude, treading heavily upon each heel of terrible Time, of Loss one cannot bring oneself to forget:

...then Poetry has just arrived??...
...Passion is a Grace that makes Existence too furiously real;
...but Passion is also a sorcerer with no care for good or evil: it is also a curse perpetually stirring and churning the feelings: ruthless and heartless, snatching away the illusion of Peace!
...longing...dear God, longing!
...Love is a filling — yet it also digs deeper, wider the moving tide of emotion...then troubles the Intellect that forever refuses the desire to ask only for peace: though such be cowardice — what care have we for blame, when we have known the terrible taste of losing oneself to a blind sensation of Severance-from-Self: one loses oneself in Love for another, while still straining to define one's own presence through the presence/absence of the beloved at one's side??
...how painful when one loses the full sovereign power to govern one's own feelings: the Void becomes a region where every trace of one's private Self vanishes without trace...and one's happiness grows dependent upon a particular Existence that is not oneself...and that separate being outside oneself is no different from oneself — like a sweet little puppy spinning in circles chasing its stubby tail, now and then chasing its own shadow, beneath the half-moon smiling its gentle forgiveness upon that game of circling round and round with itself!
...tonight...Poetry...returns again unhurriedly...and says: here, here — this direct confrontation with the dark lake of Consciousness and the ten-thousand things one wishes to forget so utterly as to become nothing but a single sorrowless blossom: Poetry says: Truth is Truth even when all is illusion, hallucination, and non-thought...yet are we so fortunate as to choose only what grants us peace of heart and stillness of mind:
...Poetry says: the wind out there is raging!
...Poetry says: the storm within our soul is rising!
...Poetry says: merely because we sit still in a warm room:
: it never means we are not like those branches tearing loose their leaves in the torrential rain of night outside!
...O Poetry: I need you,
but that does not mean I do not hate and rage at you!
is it not because humankind wished to forget the reminder that they chose the faith for which jesus was nailed to the cross??
...and so though I wish to deny and disown Poetry: I still bow in reverence and thank Poetry for granting me this way of escape: these letters made luminous — for they are the carvings of my Suffering, that Suffering which — like the pain of labor — is itself a complex Happiness of this writhing lineage of feeling-creatures called a Life of fullest meaning. What can I say before the words of the ancients: "God grants Suffering to humankind in order to complete humankind!" — when the First Noble Truth proclaimed by the Blessed Buddha is the Truth of Suffering?? How can it be exaggeration when the Blessed Buddha names this world the Saha World — dusk-mingled in all the dualities of the realm of right and wrong??
...so it is: only the one who is solitary in a loneliness come utterly undone is visited by Poetry!
...the night greets Poetry when Poetry greets the shadow of my heart broken and fallen beneath the foot of love's cross!
...Ôi,
...each moment i die a little and oh it is still the death in ecstacy: how pain can make the body feels more real than that invisible inner world!
...O Poetry! must I thank you or curse you when you make me feel too truly myself?? Oh, pain, pain, pain: the utter reality of the illusive realm of senses: and such is the triumph of life over death: true pain!
note: \"Cõi Ta-Bà\" is the Vietnamese rendering of the Sanskrit Sahā-lokadhātu — the world of endurance where beings tolerate suffering; rendered here as \"Saha World\" following standard English Buddhist usage rather than the more literal \"realm of forbearance.\"
issues: "vô-tưởng" (Buddhist: non-arising of thought) rendered as "non-thought" — faithful to Dharma terminology but less accessible than "empty thought"
"nhập-nhoạng những nhị-nguyên" — "dusk-mingled" captures the twilight-murk of nhập-nhoạng but slightly loses the literal sense of blurred/intermingled dualities
"biệt-thân" — "Severance-from-Self" is literal but unusual; "Separation" flows more naturally at the cost of losing the body (thân) resonance
The final two lines are the poet's own English (verbatim in the Vietnamese source) and are preserved exactly, including her intentional grammar ("body feels", "ecstacy")

en_body:
GEOGRAPHY OF VIETNAM

According to Memory Learned in Elementary School in Sài-Gòn Before 30-4-1975.*
...................................................................................................


General Outline: Vietnam lies in Southeast Asia, along the shores of the South Sea. Its shape curves like the letter S, with a total area of 327,500 square kilometers. Vietnam falls between latitudes 8°30' and 23°40'; longitudes 102 to 109 degrees. To the East and South, the South Sea; to the West, Laos and Cambodia; to the North, the three Chinese provinces of Quảng-Đông, Quảng-Tây, and Vân-Nam. (According to Ancient History, Quảng-Đông/Quảng-Tây once belonged to Vietnam; King Quang-Trung was preparing to reclaim them when he died.) Vietnam is widest from Lai-Châu to Móng-Cái, at 600 km; narrowest at Quảng-Trị, 40 km (from the Trường-Sơn range to Cà-Mau); longest from Nam-Quan to Mũi Cà-Mau, 1,650 km. The coastline measured at 2,200 km from Móng-Cái to Hà-Tiên, together with a land border of 2,500 km.


...................................................................................................
For the writings that follow this piece:
*Materials excerpted from the following books:
(1).. VIỆT-SỬ TOÀN-THƯ, Phạm Văn Sơn
(2).. SÀI-GÒN NĂM XƯA, Vương Hồng Sển
(3).. NGƯỜI VIỆT ĐẤT VIỆT, Cửu-Long-Giang, Toan-Ánh
(4).. CON ĐƯỜNG THIÊN-LÝ, Nguyên Hiến Lê
(5).. SAIGON XƯA VÀ NAY, Thế Uyên
(6).. VĂN-CHƯƠNG MIỆT VƯỜN, Sơn Nam
(7).. KÝ-THÁC, "Rừng Mắm", Bình Nguyên Lộc
(8).. TÂN-CHÂU, Nguyên Văn Kiểm
(9).. BẢY NGÀY TRONG ĐỒNG-THÁP, Nguyên Hiến-Lê
(10) THƯƠNG NHỚ MƯỜI HAI, Vu Bằng
note: The file title "đỊA-Là VIỆT-NAM" is a deliberate pun on "địa-lý" (geography): splitting the word and replacing "lý" with "là" (is), so it reads simultaneously as "Geography" and "The Land Is Vietnam" — an act of reclamation through grammar itself.
issues: The parenthetical '(from the Trường-Sơn range to Cà-Mau)' is geographically puzzling as written — Cà-Mau is the southern tip, not the coast at Quảng-Trị; faithfully preserved as the poet's schoolbook memory regardless.

Version B's 'South China Sea' for 'Nam-Hải' was rejected — 'Nam-Hải' (South Sea) is the pre-1975 Saigon schoolbook term and the correct referent here; 'South China Sea' is a modern Western naming convention the poet did not use.

Chinese province names rendered in Vietnamese diacritics (Quảng-Đông, Quảng-Tây, Vân-Nam) rather than pinyin (Guangdong, Guangxi, Yunnan) — this matches the Vietnamese source and the poet's own naming frame, but a reader unfamiliar with Vietnamese may not recognize these as Chinese provinces.
The title pun — đỊA-Là VIỆT-NAM vs. địa-lý (geography) — is carried in the English title 'The Land — Is Vietnam' as a near-equivalent wordplay; the pun cannot be fully reproduced in English.

en_body:
Translating Wislawa Szymborska:
VIEW WITH A GRAIN OF SAND
"The Joy of Writing"

Why does this written doe bound through these written woods?
For a drink of written water from a spring
whose surface will xerox her muffle?
Why does she lift her head? does she hear something?
Perched on four slim legs borrowed from the truth,
she pricks up her ears beneath my fingertips.


Hoàng-Ân, freely rendered: The Blessed-Vow of Creation

Why does this phantom-image doe bound freely through these
phantom-image woods?
To steep her throat with one sip of water from a brook
whose surface will xerox her discreet, velvety-rose muzzle?
Why does she raise her face so intently; could she have heard
some stirring?
Four slim legs, borrowed upon Truth, bearing her own Existence,
She pricks up her ears listening-watching beneath the tips of my fingers.

(24/02/2013)
note: \"Hạnh-Nguyện\" is a Buddhist term for the meritorious vow of a Bodhisattva — Hoàng-Ân reframes Szymborska's creative act as an act of sacred spiritual aspiration, which is the conceptual heart of her free rendering.
issues: "hư-ảnh" — phantom-image is a workable compound but "hư" carries Buddhist emptiness/unreality (śūnyatā-adjacent) that neither "phantom" nor "shadow-image" fully lands; accepted as best available
"Hạnh-Nguyện" — both syllables matter: hạnh = merit/blessed, nguyện = vow/aspiration; "Blessed-Vow" keeps the hyphenated compound matching her diction but flattens the Bodhisattva-register of nguyện
"thấm giọng" — literally to soak/permeate the throat, not merely quench it; "steep her throat" is unusual in English but is the most faithful rendering and fits her willingness to defamiliarize

en_title: Reading the Volume KINH THI VIỆT-NAM by TRƯƠNG TỬU
en_body:
(11/12/2012)

Folk poetry is oral literature. One nameless soul gives voice to it — then other nameless ones take it up in song and recitation, shaping it slowly, by chance, to fit the living conditions of their own time: until that poetry mirrors the very Spirit of an entire People. In truth, whatever the differences in geography and custom, folk poetry expresses the collective Soul of all humankind — the Will to Exist, to Endure. Vietnamese folk poetry in particular "brings into sharp relief the Spirit of Independence and the long perseverance our ancestors practiced across centuries to resist the peril of Sinicization." Then France came — wielding every means to ruin Vietnamese culture through a machinery of enforced ignorance. Through so much dâu bể, through upheaval and exile, the Vietnamese People still kept their indomitable Spirit alive through folk poetry. The role of folk poetry is immense — like a sacred national history of the common Soul: embodying the Courage that resists the foreign invader's assault upon our culture. As example, I invite you to read the following lines, excerpted from "Before Entering the Words" in the volume Kinh Thi written by Trương Tửu:

  "It was long ago now — twelve, thirteen years — when strolling to catch the breeze around Hồ Gươm, I had the honor of meeting an old man who belonged to that generation born around the time King Tự-Đức passed from this world. Hair white as morning frost, beard white as morning frost, long fingernails, domestic silk garments, Gia-Định shoes, clear-glass spectacles...he carried the bearing of an ancient Confucian scholar still lingering in the present — troubled and fretful at having to witness the in-between scenes of his homeland's changing society.
  Chance placed me beside him. Fanning himself with a peach-shaped feather fan, he gazed fixedly toward the lake, his face drawn in deep thought — and Longing. I honored the commemorative stillness of that displaced soul, so I too sat in silence watching the breeze-seekers pass along the shore.
  Then suddenly — children's voices drifted over from the edge of the octagonal pavilion...

  Chi chi trành trành
  Cái đanh thổi lửa
  Con ngựa chết trương
  Ba vương lập đế
  Ú tế đi tìm
  Hú tim bắt ập.

  Without warning, the old man asked me: 'Did you hear what those children were just singing?' Surprised, I answered respectfully, 'Yes, Elder. Why do you ask?' The old man shook his head several times, then spoke at length:

  'They sing it all wrong. The ones who taught them taught it all wrong. That song is not as they sing it. Originally it was a Prophecy — a Sấm — handed down from the ancient ancestors, only recently come to its fulfillment. The true first line is: Chu Chu Rành Rành — meaning: proclaimed openly, so that all may know. The second line is: Cái Đanh Nổ Lửa — speaking of French soldiers firing their cannon at the gates of Đà-Nẵng. The third line is: Con Ngựa Đứt Cương — pointing to the death of King Tự-Đức and the disorder of the court in those days. At that time, Tường and Thuyết held monopoly over power, killing many of the loyal and upright.
  They dared to defy even the testament of the departed king — casting Dục-Đức into a lightless prison, installing Hiệp-Hòa upon the throne. Then they killed Hiệp-Hòa and put Kiến-Phúc in his place. Then they killed Kiến-Phúc and raised Hàm-Nghi to the royal seat. Three kings succeeding one another in swift succession like that — that is why the verse reads: Ba Vương Tập Đế. In the reign of King Hàm-Nghi, Thuyết rose up against the French, was defeated, and had to carry the king away into hiding. On one hand the French army moved to suppress the uprising; on the other, they devised schemes to seize Hàm-Nghi and pacify the hearts of the people. Hence the verse: Cấp Kế Đi Tìm. In the end — because of the traitor Ngọc — the king was seized suddenly in the forest of Tuyên-Hóa while he lay sleeping. That is the meaning of the final verse: Hú Tim Òa Ập!'"
note: \"Dâu bể\" (mulberry field into open sea) is a classical Vietnamese idiom for catastrophic transformation across lifetimes — left untranslated here as the poet herself preserves untranslatable cultural sounds and phrases in her English work.
issues: "thiên quốc-sử dân-gian" — "thiên" can mean sacred/celestial or simply epic/narrative volume; rendered as "sacred national history" which captures the elevated register but is not fully certain.

"dâu bể" left untranslated (literally: mulberry field to open sea — a classical idiom for vast upheaval and transformation); kept per the poet's own practice of preserving untranslatable ritual/poetic terms, but a reader note may help.

"Trước Khi Vào Lời" rendered as "Before Entering the Words" (literal) rather than A's paraphrase "Before the Preface"; the literal reading is preferred but the phrase is a formal section header whose exact convention is ambiguous.
"nói luôn" — both A's "spoke plainly" and B's "spoke at length" are plausible; chose B's "spoke at length" as "luôn" here signals continuous/extended speech, not frankness.

en_title: The First Dream of the Kiss
en_body:
The sky is cool today — my heart wide-open as the harbor's mouth,
The dawn-light, gentle as fingers,
Playing upon the wind-strings, stirring their sound-waves,
Through the gaps of leaves, shimmering into flower-speckles.
The sparrows busily fling wide their music-wings,
The flock darts in delight, speaking love to one another...
And with lids half-drawn like a poem, breathing so lightly,
Blanket and pillow soft, still cool and smooth with Dream—
...Last night already...we had kissed each other inside it!...

(21/05/2007)
note: "Nụ hôn" in Vietnamese is literally "bud-kiss" — the kiss as an unopened blossom — a nuance neither title preserves.
issues: Line 7: 'thơ' literally means 'poetic / like poetry' — 'like a poem' is interpretive compression; the line means the half-closed eyelids have a dreamlike, verse-like quality, which 'half-drawn like a poem' captures but does not translate precisely.
Line 9: the Vietnamese ellipsis falls immediately after 'đã' (already), marking a breath of stunned recollection before the act is named; the English restructuring softens that exact pause-point.
Title: 'Nụ Hôn' carries 'nụ' (bud, as in a bud yet to bloom) — both versions lose the bud-of-a-kiss nuance, treating it as a plain noun.
Line 4: 'hoa điểm' could read 'speckled flowers' (adj+noun) rather than 'flower-speckles' (compound noun) — the compound reads more naturally in her English voice but shifts the grammar slightly.

en_body:
word word word word
word word word word
word word word word
word word word word

word word word word word
word word word word word

word word word word word word
word word word word word word word word

word word word word word word word
word word word word word word word
word word word word word word
word word word word word word word word.

(12-12-2012)
note: \"chữ\" is Vietnamese for written character / word / letter; the poem titled \"Wordless Dream\" fills every line with only the word for \"word,\" making the text itself a concrete performance of silence — the dream has no words, so all that remains is the naked sign for words.
issues: The source file contains only the Vietnamese word "chữ" (written character / word / letter) repeated in every line — no other text is present. This is almost certainly intentional: the poem titled "Wordless Dream" enacts its own thesis by consisting entirely of the word for "word," leaving the dream itself unspeakable. If "chữ" is instead a placeholder and the real poem was never committed to the file, no translation is possible without the original text.

Whether to capitalize "Word" (invoking the Biblical logos — John 1:1 — consistent with the poet's syncretic register) is the one open judgment call. Left lowercase here because "chữ" is the ordinary Vietnamese term for written character, not the theological Logos, and the conceptual force lives in the structure, not the elevation.

en_body:
O Poetry!
O Poetry...hold me close!
Rise as smoke and carry me all the way to Heaven—
weep upon the clouds until Death,
in heart-rending grief, fall into the soaring storm of rain.
O Love—
O Poetry—
O my Beloved..!!

The Hour of Eternal Farewell
O Poetry!
O Poetry...hold me close!
Rise as smoke and carry me all the way to Heaven—
weep upon the clouds until Death,
in heart-rending grief, fall into the soaring storm of rain.
O Love—
O Poetry—
O my Beloved..!!

(22/03/2007)
note: The inverted structure — stanza before the title, then title, then stanza again — is authentic to the Vietnamese source and functions as a lyric epigraph; it is retained exactly.
issues: Line 5 — 'ngút' in 'bão ngút mưa': rendered as 'soaring' (vertical, towering) but it can also carry 'overflowing / torrential'; both senses are defensible and 'soaring storm of rain' is the most musical fit.

The pre-title epigraph structure (stanza first, then title, then stanza repeated) is unusual in English — preserved faithfully from the Vietnamese layout but may read as a printing error to an unfamiliar reader.

en_body:
Grant me Thought grown humble and reverent—
To greet new Love as she tiptoes softly in.
Grant me Imagination bright and fresh,
To carry deep-aching Love into resonant song.
Grant me the knowing how to remember, long,
To welcome you into the tender shoots of first season.
Grant me to sit in stillness where you are,
Longing deep and rich for someone's fragrant laughter.

Grant a home woven of green coconut leaves,
Grant the deep-molded xu-xê cake, sweet and crisp.
That is — grant me to follow the drifting months and days,
Downstream along the Grace of blessed encounter, tending the heart's fruit.
Grant to coax Love into full blazing wakefulness—
Cheeks flushed with innocent rose, lips and eyes of crystal-light.

(22/01/2013)
note: The title `Hạnh-Phúc` means happiness/bliss; the poem is a supplicant's prayer (Xin = grant/please), braiding domestic Vietnamese imagery (xu-xê cake, coconut-leaf house) with the abstract capitalized Loves the poet herself favors in English.
issues: Line 4: `trầm-thống` (deep + grief/pain) — 'deep-aching' is interpretive; 'deep-grieving' is more literal but less musical; chosen to preserve her lyrical register.
Line 3: `xinh tươi` (pretty + fresh/vivid) — 'bright and fresh' chosen over 'bright and fair' (B) since 'fair' misses the bloom/vitality of tươi.
Line 11: The particle `Là` functions as a clarifying connector ('that is to say'); 'That is —' preserves it but slightly disrupts the anaphoric 'Grant' rhythm of the stanza.
Line 14: `nắng pha-lê` — literally 'crystal sunlight'; hyphenated 'crystal-light' compresses it; could equally read 'lips and eyes of crystal sunlight' for full literality.

en_body:
HOÀNG-NHƯ-XUÂN: 1989

(14/05/2012)

A Poem 1:
 I smoked. I smoked a poem.
 A poem I smoked
 into a poem I dreamt.

 I dreamt up a poem I smoked
 that I've smoked up a poem
 that I've dreamt that I have smoked
 upon a poem I've dreamt..!!

A Poem 2:
 Flower—Blending—Becoming—Fire...My heart flares ablaze
 Smoke lifts in rising waves
 swaying — yet the little boat
 downstream upstream downstream along the red blood-stream
 Red blood, deep-dark at the Source,
 Traveling throughout the whole body—
 As smoke it blends and wanders everywhere
 Becoming a poem without end..!!


*A handwritten poetry collection whose pen-name is the title of the collection
note: The title 'Hoàng-Như-Xuân: 1989' is the poet's pen-name and the name of a handwritten collection she made that year — the poem is a meditation on that act of writing itself, so the self-referential loop in Poem 1 (smoking a poem into a dreamt poem) is intentional and complete.
issues: Line 1 (Poem 2): 'Hoa hòa hóa hỏa' is a tonal-homophone cascade (four near-identical syllables differing only in tone marks: level→falling→rising-dipping→asking) — a sonic waterfall that is structurally untranslatable; the em-dash chain is the closest English equivalent but loses the auditory collapse into fire.
'đậm xậm nguồn' — the reduplicated intensifier (đậm xậm = very-dark-dark) at the Source has sonic weight that 'deep-dark' approximates but does not match.
'sóng-sánh' — a liquid-motion compound suggesting the visual of something swishing and rocking; 'swaying' catches the motion but not the specific liquid shimmer.

Line 7: 'Hòa khói đi về khắp' — literally 'blending-smoke goes-and-returns everywhere'; 'As smoke it blends and wanders everywhere' (B) is chosen over A's 'Blending smoke travels throughout' to avoid repeating 'throughout' from line 6, but the 'đi về' (going-and-returning) circularity is slightly softened.

en_body:
Mountains upright—
Ten fingers
raising spears and lances,
thrusting against Heaven,
Flags flutter and fly,
flinging wide the wind,
The bronze censer gleaming,
spreading streams of incense,
billowing smoke and cloud.

The People return, return to one another—
The answering voice
resounds like thunder,
rebounds into mountains,
echoes through every cell of Air,
transforms into a Proclamation of Humanity—
goes to rebuild the Foundation,
brings warmth and fullness, Independence,
for the People's Nation under the Southern Sky!
And peace upon the world—
All of Humanity, one Heart!

(29/04/2012)
note: Hồng-Bàng is the name of the legendary founding dynasty of Vietnam (~2879–258 BCE), whose progenitors are the Dragon Lord Lạc Long Quân and the Mountain Fairy Âu Cơ — invoking it here frames the poem as a call to reclaim primordial national identity and human dignity at once.
issues: "cơ-đồ" (line 16): rendered as "Foundation" — could also be "Realm" or "Heritage"; the compound means foundational enterprise/patrimony, neither English word fully captures it.

"hịch nhân-bản" (line 15): "Proclamation of Humanity" is the best available rendering; "nhân-bản" carries a humanist-philosophy sense (human nature, human dignity) slightly narrower than "Humanity" as a collective noun.

"ấm no" (line 17): "warmth and fullness" is the most literal (ấm = warm, no = satiated/fed); "abundance" (Version B) is more elevated but drifts from the material, bodily image of enough food and warmth.

"Dân Quốc trời Nam" (line 18): "People's Nation under the Southern Sky" — Dân-Quốc is a compressed compound for the Vietnamese people's polity; the Southern Sky (trời Nam) is a classical Vietnamese self-reference that both versions preserve correctly.

en_title: When the Moth-Kind Spreads Wings of Wildly-Drunken Fire
en_body:
...When Night offers its honey so sweetly ripened in foolish Love: the fireflies kindle their own hearts awake, flock by flock, swarms drifting languidly—dazedly frantic toward that perfectly round Light of childlike Love—entering the Hope of heaven-knowing, destiny-fulfilled: a small offering with all its wholeness of naive innocence, that even a solitary dream of giving and receiving alone is enough to stir awake the Waiting Soul through layer upon layer of stars in the high and silent sky, laden with the suppressed Vow of longing-watch..!!

 ...Night restless and tossing-turning since Creation first woke its hunger-thirst—craving to beg for a containing, nourishing embrace of those pent-up spiritual anguishes hidden deep within a frenzied-mad Consciousness waiting to surrender wholly into Rose-Pink Love! ...To search into that dreamlike velvet hue is a Season of Love that soaks the whole body complete—from embracing the pillow of Beloved-Tenderness then bursting into divine awakening that greets sky and earth with dazzled innocence, with blazing-blazing Light, with the ardent warmth of the circle of Yearning-Love nurturing to the uttermost every dream drenched in pearl-tears and every thought so longing, so waiting—to the moment of Revelation, the countless and ultimate meanings of understanding those deeply-bound bindings the poetic soul longs to wrench free and escape.. so that finally there is the vast and wide-open Blessed Vow: a first embrace, and the absolute-final salvation of Love-Affair...

 ...And the Heart-Light of Love, ten-thousand-divine-manifest from the very Beginning, now opens its eyes and bursts forth in the resonant word of Forgiveness: the Moths blinded in the wildly-arrogant earliest Awakening—their layered wing-beat sounds rising to raise up the Season of Passion-Love, soothing whoever in this life writhes restlessly with each heartbeat's invitation, containing the grief-soul earnestly waiting through life, searching and struggling for the Vermilion Heart! The orchestra of hot blood—the source of Consciousness drenched and drenched through—releases and suddenly shoots forth a grand symphony dizzily swaying between purple and deep-viridian of tender longing, fragrant-spreading in the rhythm of the Moths' arching wings, in the silent melody of frenzied Love.. into the First Season, soaring absolutely the darkly-obscure Love-Affair, dizzily stacked upon every vein of eternal Light—from the time the tender Heart had never yet witnessed Passion-Love yet already knew in silence to listen-watch for the eternal Final Season of Love's register.. The silent chest bears witness—bears witness only to the words from the deep bottom of the lotus-heart, through the spinning dance of the kind drunk on Light: the nameless ones merging into one Loving kind, in the deeply-pooled stream of compassion-prayers petitioning the stream of Warm Fire from the Rose Heart, ivory-and-jade—Eternal Love that can hold all ruin... Beside the pockmarked temples crumbling in bricks and stone from so many hasty and makeshift wars: the warm hearth caged within the Heart blazes its tongue eternally in the silent reality of the first-and-final season of victory—the singular Revelation of those infinitely-drunk on Light, making a lesson of shimmering meaning like the meditative Stars marking the chest that holds the silent calling: a tender care, nourishing every solitary and gallant private longing that breaks loose from all useless obstacles..!!

 ...Yet still the Stars, each one, contain Light and bestow it endlessly in their silent stillness of sweetly-overwhelming tender affection—with the glorious-ascending beauty of those young ones who know how to Love Valiantly and Gracefully from their own Heart-Source, echoing back from millions upon billions of seasons of light, a thousand distances away on the arc of Dream beyond all capacity to perceive—still ardently calling in wild-mad devotion like the wellspring of Life eternally urging the Moth-Kind in their restless stirring, from the divine condensation whispering since the watery-egg—through a sleep that seems still yet trembles from its root—the Yearning for Light is the testimony of Life, most manifestly luminous.. And a thousand stars resonate across the region of Love's Thunder-Lightning, calling deep into the dark obscurity of every sound-element of every utterly-blind strand of Moth—carried and sustained by the bat's sonar-color resonant with knowledge that transcends the Grace forgiving all mistakes that cause despair! Still the Stars stand motionless, bestowing Grace to the very uttermost of existence, utterly transcending every boundary of mad obsession: and grafting into the Moth-Kind—those who carry at last the entanglement of definition-and-judgment, not yet entered into the Final Season of Passion-Love's melancholy meanings—the Moth-Kind soars after the Twin-Wings-of-Fire-and-a-Thousand-Stars, rising ecstatic into the Season of Light, and the divine soul suddenly breaks free and is born into the tonal arc of the Musical Score of the first Glorious Season of Light—warmly rosy, beautiful: the Celestial-Body of the Garden of Eden embracing into the countless spirit-essences hungering and waiting, the pure selfless Yearning of Love's Call, by the velvet cradle of night's hue waiting to be recalled to the Source of Sacred Fire: the life of the Moths smolders quietly in each settling sediment, in the bittersweet-chill of each string of the instrument—the sound of wings rushing-drunk on the life-existence of the Moth, manifestly divine..!!

(31/12/2005)
note: \"Thiêu-Thân\" (moth, literally burn-body) and \"đom-đóm\" (firefly) are distinct: fireflies appear in stanza 1 kindling their own hearts; Moths (Thiêu-Thân, who burn themselves in flame) enter in stanza 3 — this distinction is load-bearing for the poem's movement from innocent longing to total self-immolation in Love.
issues: "mầu mắt loài dơi âm-vang chất hiểu-biết" — the bat's sonar-color-as-knowledge is extremely compressed; "bat's sonar-color resonant with knowledge" approximates the image but the original may carry a more specific phenomenological sense of echolocation-as-sight

"hạnh-nguyện bao-la thênh-thang" — "hạnh-nguyện" is a Buddhist felicitous vow; rendered as "Blessed Vow" loses some of the specifically Buddhist doctrinal weight (it is a vow of merit/happiness, closer to bodhisattva vow than Christian blessing)
"bếp ấm lồng Tim" — "lồng" oscillates between cage (confinement) and housed-within (nesting); "caged within the Heart" leans toward enclosure, which may or may not match the poet's intent

"Tim non chưa từng chứng-tích Ái-Tình đã biết âm-thầm nghe-ngóng" — the paradox of the young Heart that never witnessed Love yet already knew in silence to wait — difficult to render the temporal impossibility without over-explaining

en_title: When Means and End Become One
en_body:
Since being discharged from the convalescent home — where body and spirit might rest and recover — and moving into the new house, I let go of the effort to keep my mind active, to resist being absorbed into the indolent, drifting ways of those still living at the facility. After a long time always bracing against the stress-laden demands of old family ties and friendships, and of circumstance...I lived in silence like a lay-contemplative in retreat: no longer keeping company with friends of every race, those boisterous gatherings heavy with alcohol and drugs and psychedelic music; finding warmth only among the near-kin on both paternal and maternal sides of my two children. Moving into the new house was like a miracle — my body and spirit grew quiet and asked for attention only to convalesce and tend themselves. I read little. I wrote even less.

 Like my whole and complete existential being sinking down and drifting, flowing like a small brook through a meadow in a young forest...I went in search of meaning for my own actions — even the creative acts: writing prose, making poems, painting, listening to music. I wanted to understand clearly what drove me to use the forms of Art in my life. Since childhood, the artistic activities had come to me very easily. From sixth grade in Việt-Nam through my university years in the United States, I was always elected to lead the journalism committee. Even the paternal grandfather and maternal grandmother of my two children each took pride in showing off my creations in English and in Vietnamese to their families and friends. Except for fourth grade and second grade, I was always chosen by my teachers as their beloved pupil — what Americans call the "teacher's pet": not only inside the classroom but beyond the school gates. My teachers in Việt-Nam often came to visit my parents' home and praised wholeheartedly that I was truly a "complete" little girl: for gentle I could be, and quick-tongued I could be. But above all, the dear teachers were captivated by my artistic creations because their form was fresh and lovely and their content ran deep. Even when I left Sài-Gòn for Vũng-Tàu on the twenty-second of April, nineteen seventy-five, to flee from Việt-Nam — once I had settled in America and the communist government of Việt-Nam permitted correspondence with those still inside the country — my teachers wrote letters to me in America. And my American teachers especially: from autumn 1975 until now they still keep in contact with me quite regularly. I am no prodigy; but the dear teachers all held affection and deep fondness for me, always encouraging me to know how to polish this small gift of mine — above all, those who cherished Art.

 ...Yet throughout these two past months, I began to reflect more deeply on the definition of Art within me: I put forth the hypothesis that Art holds value as an objective realm — but if so, then what is the relationship between myself, who uses Art to express my inner world and sensibility, and that objective realm? And how do I assess the bond between myself and Art: as a creator — does Art lie latent and corresponding within my own existential being, or outside me? When I bring forth my creations, am I myself Art?

 The psychologist Maslow proposed a triangular diagram for the development of a secure and complete life: the foundation of this triangle is material sufficiency — food enough, warmth of clothing — then the safety of body and spirit, then the intimate relationships necessary to build self-confidence and self-fulfillment, and only at the very apex does one arrive at the self-creation of one's spiritual dimension. As the archaeologist Arthur Evans in the early nineteen hundreds excavated the ancient Minoan city beside the city of Knossos — and both humanists and archaeologists agreed with Evans that it was the first city of the Western world to have settled in prosperity enough to create for itself a civilized culture — those high works of Art made with such effort to adorn the landscape of their living.

 As we know, "Art as Means or End" has been a subject examined, discussed, and even fiercely debated perhaps since Art first appeared within human society. One school grounds Art firmly upon the system of văn dĩ tải đạo — "literature to carry the Way." The opposing school teaches the principle of "art for art's sake."

 ...And after two long months of reflection and searching, I find myself thinking: when I was still a small child I swam in the sounds of the Ả Đào and Ca Trù my Father sang each early morning as he sat before his water-pipe crock and pot of tea; in the sweet voice of Mother chanting poetry beneath the warm roof that Father and Mother had raised for us three sisters; in the street-vendor calls of the itinerant peddlers through the dreamy lanes of childhood; in the festival gatherings when Father and Mother and uncles and elder brothers sat splitting bamboo and rattan to weave lanterns great and small, organizing the grandest Mid-Autumn Festival of all the neighboring lanes; in the festive bustle of preparing for Tết Nguyên Đán — when Father sat on the divan in the ancestral hall amid trays of soaking glutinous rice, ground mung bean paste, fatty pork, stacked dong leaves and rattan ties for wrapping bánh chưng, while the aunts and elder sisters busied themselves trimming, drying, and cooking the sweet preserves...in the lẩy Kiều the old neighbor grandmother chanted to lull her grandchild to sleep; in the elegant music of the cải-lương operas, the hát bội and hát chèo drifting into the house from the neighbor's television each evening.

 ...And then the faltering and dreaming of girlhood...captivated by reading prose and poetry, by gazing at paintings and photographs; captivated by copying out poems, transcribing music scores, drawing...secretly waiting for Mother to leave the house to practice singing...

 ...In those days, Art was only a pleasure like all the other games of childhood: it clung to life the way clothes cling to skin. Practicing to write prose, to make poems — like writing compositions or reading dictation passages aloud for the class to copy. And as I grew, on into the middle years I now inhabit, Art has remained a means to release (release) and to express the undercurrents of feeling that crystallize up from the depths of the lake of consciousness.

 ...Now...

 ...I suspect that...from using Art as a means to know myself, until the third eye — the Wisdom-Eye — suddenly opened to see: Art is an objective realm, present and vast, suffused throughout Existence, whose very framework is the principle of Truth-Goodness-Beauty...when I crossed beyond the threshold of the self and awakened before that luminous land called Art, I in turn became a means for Art to illuminate the Wisdom-Eye of my fellow beings. And I suspect that what the Buddhist doctrine of No-Self means to me is: to enter the vast and boundless void in order to attain the realization of No-Self — to achieve Self — then to dissolve into the Great Body entire. When the Wisdom-Eye breaks open for the creative self and I truly recognize the vast luminous Light that is Art, when I draw heart-silk from deep within the hidden core of my existential being: in childhood I was the silkworm drawing silk to spin the cocoon; in youth, living and dying with my passion for Art, I lay within the cocoon like a Zen master seeking the answer to the koan of Art. And throughout these two months I have been gnawing at the cocoon...Oh, how I long for when I shall be like a beautiful butterfly emerging from the cocoon and soaring into the vast, golden-palace realm of what is truly sacred — the world luminous and alive in its manifestation...Ah, so that is it: am I not beginning to directly experience Zhuangzi's meditation: "Am I the person who becomes the butterfly — or am I brought into being by a butterfly dreaming me into the person that is I?"

(19/08/2007)
note: Văn dĩ tải đạo is a classical Confucian-Vietnamese literary principle meaning literature as the vehicle of moral/ethical Way — left in Vietnamese with gloss, as the poet herself uses it untranslated in the original.
issues: The compound 'minh-mung' (in 'ánh sáng minh-mung') is rendered as 'vast luminous Light' — the word may mean diffuse-bright or haze-bright; neither version resolves it definitively.

The Zhuangzi closing line is syntactically unusual in Vietnamese ('ta là do bướm mơ thành người là ta'); my synthesis ('brought into being by a butterfly dreaming me into the person that is I') is faithful but slightly awkward — Zhuangzi's standard English rendering differs enough to cause tension.
Both prior translators dropped 'Mother' (Mẹ) from the Mid-Autumn festival passage ('Bố Mẹ và chú, cậu, anh...'); I restored her, which is the correct reading but departs from both received versions.

The phrase 'đắc-chứng vô-ngã để đạt ngã' (attain No-Self to achieve Self) is a compressed Buddhist dialectic that resists clean English syntax; the em-dash I used signals the paradox but does not resolve it.

en_title: Confucius and Laozi: Two Political Paths
en_body:
(26/01/2013)

"How many times the mulberry-fields have sunk, the sea has risen —
 Jade remnants, fallen gold: pages turned before the lamp.
 Ten thousand paths: where now to seek a heaven and earth made strange?
 A thousand ancients return — the Sages grown familiar once again.
 Brilliant flowers: the brush-tip traces its fresh vermillion lines.
 The inkstone's essence sets a stone-firm foundation.
 Present and ancient — separated by two shores of generation —
 Empathy together bridges the rhythm of connection."

 (Đông Hồ: "Đề Từ")


 Confucian learning leans toward the imposition of Discipline as the skeletal framework of a social polity. Within it, Ethics is defined as the restraint and governance of the impulses — of emotion and of action — among society's members. Like all efforts to seek a model for the maintenance of human society, Confucian learning holds its share of beneficial essences: among them, the Way of Human Relations. We may assign to that machinery of political thought the name: "Governance."

 An illustration of this Governance is found in the tale "Tử-Sản Instructs His Son in the Art of Ruling the People," from the TẢ THỊ XUÂN THU of Tả Khâu Minh, court historian of the state of Lỗ — a man who upheld Confucian learning: King Trịnh Tử-Sản of the state of Trịnh lay gravely ill — between life and death — and summoned his son Thái-Thúc to his bedside to impart his final wishes: "When I am gone, you shall ascend the throne, tending the people and guarding the realm in my stead. Only one of great Virtue may employ a policy of Clemency to win the hearts of men. Otherwise, nothing surpasses a policy of Strictness. Laws that are yielding — the people grow insolent and easily perish. Laws that are strict and harsh, fierce as fire — the people fear them, and fewer die by fire."

 When Trịnh Tử-Sản passed from this world, Thái-Thúc rose to power and, unable to bring himself to be severe, ruled with Clemency. The people could not restrain themselves — they fell into contention over private interests; thieves and brigands arose, and disorder spread everywhere. Thái-Thúc, in remorse, confessed aloud: "Had I known sooner to heed my father's words, we would not have come to this!" He tightened the laws of the realm and led his armies forth to suppress the unrest. The king re-established the order of Governance over the people; the state of Trịnh was restored to peace.

 Confucius praised thus: A policy of Clemency renders the people lax; when lax, firm and forceful law must be employed to restore Order. Forceful measures harm the people; when the people are harmed, Clemency is again enacted — and so Governance finds its Harmony.

(to be continued)
note: The opening epigraph is a poem by Đông Hồ (Lâm Tấn Phác, 1906–1969), a Southern Vietnamese poet and scholar; "Đề Từ" means "Preface" or "Epigraph," marking this as a quoted verse prologue rather than Hoàng-Ân's own lines.
issues: Line 5 of epigraph: 'Anh-hoa' carries a specific sense of literary/cultural splendor — 'Brilliant flowers' captures the image but loses the implied sense of civilizational flowering; 'Splendid flowers' or 'Literary brilliance' would be more precise but less musical.
Line 8 of epigraph: 'nhịp giao-liên' literally means 'the beat/rhythm of liaison/intercourse/connection' — 'rhythm of connection' is faithful in spirit but softens the sense of active linking.

'thứ-sử' for Tả Khâu Minh: rendered as 'court historian' (B), which is the commonly accepted gloss for 左丘明's role, though 'prefect' (A) is also defensible; the Vietnamese does not resolve this ambiguity.
Deathbed speech: 'trăn-trối' means dying final wishes/instructions, not 'teaching' — both versions are corrected to 'final wishes' in this synthesis.

Final Confucius summary: 'chính-sự an-hòa' is rendered 'Governance finds its Harmony' — slightly interpretive; the Vietnamese literally means 'governance-affairs are at peace/harmony' without the personification.

en_body:
Those stones, those stones—
breaking through the strata of Sound,
falling into the still face of the water,
disturbing Silence into rings.

Those round rhythms, resonant—
Dusk within the golden eyes,
blazing its invitation to Death:
a gentle Decision,
a gentle Inevitability.

The dinosaur cranes its neck back,
gazes upon the lake's face as it wakens—
the sound that stirs all Space,
shatters Time and returns as echo.

The Panic — immeasurable —
is the Action no one foresaw:
the dinosaur's foot scatters the stone,
the stone falls into the lake.

(29/04/2012)
note: The poem enacts causality in reverse: the catastrophic Terror (hoảng-hốt vô-cùng) is revealed only at the end to be an accident — a foot grazing a stone — which recasts the blazing, Death-inviting grandeur of stanzas 1–3 as the unintended consequence of a trivial act.
issues: "nhịp tròn âm-vang" (stanza 2, line 1): "nhịp" = rhythm/beat, not ripple — "Those round rhythms, resonant" is faithful to the word but leaves open whether the poet means the visual ring-ripples described as rhythmic, or purely acoustic pulses; the ambiguity may be intentional.
"ngoái cổ" implies turning the neck backward specifically — "cranes its neck back" (from A) preserves this directional nuance that B's bare "cranes its neck" loses.
"dạt" (stanza 4, line 3) = sweep aside / push away — "scatters" is close but slightly stronger than the original gesture of the foot grazing the stone.
Shift to present tense in stanza 4 ("scatters", "falls") is a translator's choice not marked in the Vietnamese; it creates immediacy but is not strictly demanded by the original.

en_body:
A Letter To You
(26/05/2007)

Dear Kim,

 Thank you for those lines sharing the deep and hidden thoughts of a Dear Friend. I received your letter last week, and still — until this very hour — I read it over and again; I treasure it as one who has found an exquisite seashell upon the shore, as lyrical and resonant as the music that rises from the heart of a sea-shell: what songs from the depth it sings! The more I read, the more the world of your letter deepens and opens into a realm of overflowing Source! It is dense with Ideas; with the horizons of Art; with the great propositions of the human realm — Love, the Immortality of the Soul, the striving of Art to penetrate beyond the visible limits of spiritual life. Your letter is beautiful as a tapestry woven by the mythic hands of the Poet. With only a few spare strokes, Kim has sketched a latent longing — "...autumn rain in the forest..." Why a forest in the autumn rain? A forest holds so many metaphors, does it not, Kim? That image already carries so much Poetry and such enchanting qualities. If Poetry could move you so deeply that you called sick leave — took the day off simply to watch the rain — then that lingering, circling reverie must have stirred in you, my Dear Friend, a Happiness both very deep and wonderfully reckless.

 Kim — surely you have felt Loneliness and Aloneness before, have you not? As a Poet, how could one not feel those deep-permeating emotions, Kim? Of late, I have longed to go alone, deep into the desert — to lie face-up upon the ever-shifting sands and confide my heart to the starry sky. In the deep and fathomless night, with only the wind's lullaby and the murmur of grains of sand touching one another — I would receive back the echo of my own soul, reflected from stars that still glimmer though they may have died a million light-years ago already. It seems I am still forever journeying in the search for the separate imprint of the Self (self-knowledge)...Perhaps Kim too has wrestled with the Abstract — with concepts, with principles, with the deeper meaning of all things and all that is...Oh! When will my mind consent at last to be still — to lie sleeping beneath the golden-flowering tree, as the ancients did. Sometimes I long for absolute rest, for a time free of worry and of wrestling with thoughts that seem to have no practical use at all.

 "...Let us greet each other in the middle of the road
 Spring before us, Eternity behind..."
 That is the Eternity and the Spring of Bùi Giáng. In Hoàng-Ân there cannot be a capacity as vast as the Master — but the Spring that Hoàng-Ân yearns for must carry some fragrance of Bùi Giáng's humanist Spring: a Spring of Loving-Kindness where each person may be loved and may love, in boundless Faith. Modern and Postmodern works of art so often lead Hoàng-Ân to think of the Babel-walls of suspicion and fear that divide person from person. To Hoàng-Ân there is nothing more unbearable than when we must restrain our Love. (witholding our Love.)

 Here, Spring is turning itself toward Summer. The wild Plum and the wild Pear, and those white-blossomed trees, bloom soft across the sky like a virgin's silken robe. The cool air and rain make the trees sprout new buds; dandelions bloom abundantly golden, and when the wind returns it releases silken tufts that spread their wings to fill the courtyard. Rows of pink Orchids blazing on the sunny days — the wind unhurried, the golden light as clear as honey, lustrous and gleaming, lifting the blue sky ever higher...Is that not already a Spring that can warm the heart and overflow with Hope, Kim?

 "...Though you come and then must go, I give my Gratitude to you..." (TCS)

 Even if Kim must one day drift far away, I will carve into my life every poetic trace Kim has left there — and I give you my Gratitude! Grateful for this portrait full of Passion — a little severe with itself, yet graceful as a watercolor wash of Spring rain in the forest...The Rain and the leaf-drenched sky of Poetry!

 Spring has come to the sky, where are you now, my Love?
 Leaves change color on the eaves, my heart grows sorrowful —
 The crescent moon herds the wind, the clouds herd smoke,
 The small blossom — aching at heart — gently opens wide,
 Catching the blue-green raindrops, rounding into a jade bud,
 Kissing the night, tilting its petals amid intoxicating fragrance,
 Erasing the sky-road, trees lace their broken boughs —
 Human feet press on their steps, cloud-feet press the sky...
 ("Spring Cloud Sorrow": Hoàng-Ân)

 ...Who is patient enough to bend the forest trees with longing, Kim? Thank you once more for gifting the dream-world of both Targo and of Kim, as Spring slowly ripens into Summer! Dear Friend, you have made beautiful the living-world of Hoàng-Ân — do you know that? Bloom fully now, those lovely blossoms — as the season of Poetry too ripens through all of Nature!
note: Bùi Giáng (1926–1998) was a renowned South Vietnamese poet and philosopher; his verse is quoted in the original, and "Cụ" is an honorific for a revered elder — "the Master" approximates but understates the filial respect implied.
issues: "cành chiết" (line 7 of embedded poem) — literally bent/air-layered boughs from horticulture; 'broken boughs' is faithful to the snapped sense but softens the propagation metaphor; 'bowed boughs' is softer and plausible but less literal
"niềm tiềm-ẩn" — rendered 'latent longing' (a felt state); 'hidden depth' (Version A) is also defensible as the sketch reveals depth not just yearning
"Cụ" for Bùi Giáng — 'the Master' is respectful but Cụ carries an elder/venerable-one connotation that no single English word fully captures
"bất-nhẫn" — 'unbearable' is standard but the Vietnamese root bất-nhẫn implies a moral inability to endure (non-forbearance), slightly stronger than the English renders
"called sick leave" preserved from her own English in the source — normalized in both versions; restored here to honor her original phrasing

en_title: A Breath of Wind, Breathing the Air of Freedom
en_body:
Let others keep their scarves and garments trim —
One lamp, one shadow — I keep company with verse.
Let others seek the city's splendor and its lure —
I tend white candles, burn incense — let remembrance ring.
    Moon tilting, tilting —
    Night wind, hushed and still,
    Stirring the veil of ngâu blossoms —
    One shadow tilts, trembling in the fragrance.
Let others chase their karmic ties and fated bonds —
Four walls hold up the roof;
I hang my rose-red Heart
And hear within the distant barking of dogs
The Poetic Soul — of orange and cinnamon — homeland love, blood running warm.
Both feet upon level ground,
The longing heart turns back — offering a breath of Goodwill into the world..!!
Let others shoulder the debts of rice and cloth —
I wander, unhurried, through the Red Dust of this world — seeking Poetry.
Let others land face-down / face-up in misfortune and luck —
I sit, beating the rhythm of a True Heart — calling to my own soul..!!
Let others play their halfway games —
Floating clouds, adrift — the shadow of a horse flashing past the outer threshold.
Let others part their ways — going / returning —
Behind the closed door-frame, I wait for the Love within..!!

(13/01/2013)
note: Line 21: "bóng câu" is a classical Sino-Vietnamese allusion to Zhuangzi's white horse flashing past a crack in the wall — time vanishing in an eyeblink; and "hồi-hướng" (line 15) carries the Buddhist meaning of dedicating accumulated merit back to all beings.
issues: "hoài-ngân" (line 4): rendered as "let remembrance ring" — captures the sonic-emotional quality but the compound means specifically nostalgic resonance/chime; slightly interpretive
"hồi-hướng" (line 15): the Buddhist technical sense of "dedication of merit" to all beings is present but muted in "turns back"; the line is richer in Vietnamese
"cam quế" (line 13): "orange and cinnamon" is literally correct but loses the specific village-scent world those fruits carry in Vietnamese memory

Line 11 "I hang my rose-red Heart" is deliberately surreal in the original (the image of nailing one's heart to four walls); both versions add qualifiers — keeping the stark enjambment into line 12 preserves the jolt

en_title: Snared in Meaning, Trapped in Word..!!
en_body:
MẮC Ý KẸT NGÔN..!!

We are those who love to measure and weigh —
Words-and-Meanings, Ideas-and-Expressions...
Sometimes.. I think to myself:
The Ancients also strained to slip free of
those entangling nets!
And so...
How many sutras, how many canons,
ancient tales,
have
come into the world!
The difficulty lies right here,
as I myself have lived it:
when I
want
you to understand me clearly enough
to love me truly
fully...
when...
I say to you:
"I love you!"

(16/12/2005)
note: \"Kinh, điển\" are Buddhist canonical terms (sutras and classical commentaries); keeping \"sutras\" and \"canons\" preserves the specifically Buddhist doctrinal weight the poet intends, distinct from generic \"scriptures.\"
issues: "so-đo" is closer to "compare and reckon" than "measure and weigh" — both versions use scale-imagery that works poetically but softens the social/comparative nuance of the word

"Em yêu Anh!" carries gendered pronoun intimacy (Em = younger/smaller self addressing an elder beloved, Anh = older male beloved) that collapses to a plain "I love you" in English — the tenderness of the pronoun hierarchy is irrecoverable without a footnote
Line 15: "Anh hiểu rõ đủ" has no explicit object in Vietnamese; adding "me" is an English necessity, not an infidelity, but worth flagging
"cố vuột" — "cố" is effortful/stubborn striving, "vuột" is the slipping-away; "strained to slip free of" (from B) captures this best but the compound is slightly awkward in English

en_body:
I love you — mine is such a pitiful Love, oh Anh!
My Longing ripened soft through seasons of crisp persimmon
Space too vast, brimming full with the scent of wind
Time counts out its intervals, the hours of distance...
Even if Your Love exists or does not —
my Love has already been fulfilled:
Salt and Ginger, salty and sharp:
From now on, I ask to remember you all my life
From now on, I am blessed to remember you — all my life!

(13/12/2006)
note: "Muối gừng mặn cay" alludes to a Southern Vietnamese folk song — salt and ginger as emblems of a plain, faithful love that endures precisely because it asks for nothing grand.
issues: "tội-nghiệp" (line 1) carries warm self-deprecating tenderness in Vietnamese — "pitiful" is exact but risks sounding coldly dismissive in English; "poor" is softer but less precise.
"nẫu chín" (line 2) means overripe-softened, not just ripened — the quality of something that has gone past crisp into yielding softness is compressed but not fully rendered.
"Anh" retained as vocative address rather than translated to "my Beloved" (her own English preference) — kept because it is the poem's intimate fulcrum and culturally irreducible.
"đã đặng" (line 6) is Southern dialect for "đã được" — "fulfilled" captures the sense of attainment but loses the dialectal color of the original.

en_body:
Unfurling the Dream-sails, I let myself drift along the sweeping current —
The whole world as though only I alone exist.
Sky and water, vast and boundless, spreading wide the wings of wind,
The blue Earth awash in golden sunlight.
Drifting lazily in fellowship with the seabirds,
And sea-fish frolicking beside the hull of the boat —
O Wind, O Wind — how wide and gentle your path of flight!
Lifting our soul into the vast and boundless Dream of Freedom!
Waves of a thousand open seas drifting and floating, linking the five continents,
The nine-cloud road — a wanderer's dream of drifting free,
The clouds unmindful of the warm breath of Love.

Twilight gently leads us back into the bay,
To sleep so sweetly beneath the diamond-net of stars —
No longer tossing before the cradle of the world's sorrow,
Dreaming of a smile to kindle bright the Dream of the Rising Sun.
Whether upon the face of the earth or in the sky of clouds and water,
Dreaming of a day the whole world shall know Great Peace —
I shall no longer be alone in this waiting,
Together we shall invite one another — unfurl the Dream-sails and set out to sea.

(20/06/2012)
note: "chiêu-dương" (line 15) is a Sino-Vietnamese compound for the rising/morning sun — a messianic dawn image that braids Buddhist and redemptive symbolism, central to this poet's syncretic register.
issues: Line 4: 'xanh' is both blue and green in Vietnamese — 'blue Earth' fits the maritime/cosmic viewpoint but 'green Earth' is equally valid; neither translator agrees.
Line 8: 'ta' is grammatically ambiguous singular/plural — 'our soul' anticipates the communal ending but the solitary reverie may still be in force here; 'my soul' is equally defensible.

Line 11: the pivot to melancholy ('clouds unmindful of Love') reads slightly abrupt as a stanza-closing coda in English — this is structural to the Vietnamese and cannot be smoothed without distortion.

en_title: A Day Within the Fragrant Arms
en_body:
(20/05/2012)

Sing out, morning bird...
Spread wings toward the sunflower field
Golden-fragrant, blending color with honey-light
Lifting the blue of the sky up high
Up high..!!
   so Space may soar
   so Time may waltz
My heart thrashes wild against its cage
My arms hold Passion the way
   Life-Force holds...

Sing out, dusk-bird
Gliding one arc of day/night, home to the nest.
Blazing orange, swooning violet,
The sun, jubilant, finds its way
   beyond that edge of the horizon...
The earth tenderly lulls,
Night's shadow silently enfolds:
   This is the hour we meet
   In dreams so full
   of tenderness
   of
   the Beloved..!!
note: Quỳ (line 2) is the sunflower or heliotrope — the sun-tracking flower — whose golden turning mirrors the bird's arc and the poem's day-to-dusk structure.
issues: "vàng ngát" carries both color depth and fragrance — "golden-fragrant" is a synesthetic reading that honors the title's "thơm" (fragrant) thread, but a case can be made for "deep gold" as simpler.

"vơ lồng" in line 8 may be a variant spelling of "vỡ lồng" (break free of the cage) or a dialectal particle; "thrashes wild against its cage" captures the urgency either way but the exact shade is uncertain.
"ôm-đồm đam-mê" — ôm-đồm implies a slightly ungainly or all-encompassing embrace; "hold Passion the way" smooths this; no English word quite matches the compounded excess of ôm-đồm.
"mến thương" rendered as "tenderness" (singular noun) rather than "tender love" — loses the two-character compound but gains abstraction consistent with her capitalized-noun voice.

en_body:
(...a poem of gratitude for the tender regard that
 Poet Chân-Trời-Chân-Mây bestowed
 upon H.A., offered to Brother and Sister
