 Cát-Đằng)...................................
....................................................


TULIPS four lips
 but
 one mouth


UẤT-KIM-HƯƠNG

 four lips
 yet
 one
 bud

.....................................................
note: The poem is bilingual by design: "TULIPS" (English, already in the source) exploits the phonetic pun two-lips, while "UẤT-KIM-HƯƠNG" (the Vietnamese name for tulip, left untranslated) answers it — four lips / one bud — the two languages mirroring each other across the page.
issues: "nụ" carries a secondary resonance of "nụ hôn" (a kiss) that "bud" cannot hold — though "bud" (flower bud) is the primary meaning and the correct choice here.
"lòng ưu-ái": chose "tender regard" (Version A) over "warm affection" (Version B) as more elevated and literary, matching her register — either is defensible.
Version B replaced "UẤT-KIM-HƯƠNG" with "TULIP" — this flattens the poem's deliberate bilingualism; the Vietnamese name is kept untranslated as in the original.

en_body:
...tears of stone,
 wood-ear
...ears sprouting from rounded river-pebbles
 eyes seeping mud-blood
deep-steeped:
 the person very lucid
 lying reversed
head arched back touching the sky
head boring spiral into the earth

...suddenly,
 mad:
books and satchel to school,
 into the office........
A lô..!!
...give three tons of firewood
burn the bare trees clean:
...a lô..??!!


(UH, central campus:
houston, tx: 22011981)
note: Version A wrongly translates \"A lô\" as \"Hello\" — this is the Vietnamese telephone greeting (from French allô), a specific cultural marker of the office-world intrusion; it must stay as \"A lô\" / \"a lô\" exactly as the poet wrote it in the original.
issues: "ears sprouting from rounded river-pebbles" — the surreal spatial relation in "tai mọc cuội tròn" is ambiguous: ears growing OUT OF pebbles (like mộc-nhi fungus from wood) vs. ears shaped like pebbles vs. pebbles acting as ears; the fungus parallel from line 2 suggests the former, but the image stays slippery
"boring spiral into the earth" for "đâm xoáy đất" — "boring" (drilling) is precise but risks the dull-reading in English; "plunging spiral" (A) is safer but loses the rotational drive of "xoáy"

"the person very lucid" vs. "wide awake" — "tỉnh" covers both sober/clear-minded and physically awake; "very lucid" keeps the intensifier "rất" and the ironic charge (lucid yet upside-down), preferred here but debatable

en_title: She — Xang Xế Cống Xang Xừ — She Departs
en_body:
Who will gather for me a grave-mound full—
I return to chip the stone, to carve a line of jade stele:
Here rests a maiden,
Without name, without age,
  for she was a tree.

A green tree while she lived upon this earth,
Each year pouring her leaves down to the wild shore of clouds—
A thousand waterfalls echoing their sound for ten thousand years,
She-the-tree shed her hair slanting, mirrored across many shores.

Now she has already found her Peace—
The waterfall no longer aches, but the Radiance of Dawn remains.
The tree no longer lulls its leaves down into the deep forest.
She no longer pours her tears upon a thousand Loves and Graces...

(University Of Houston, Central Campus,
 Houston, TX, USA: Hạ 1981)
note: Xang Xế Cống Xang Xừ are syllables of Vietnamese traditional music solfège (nhạc tài tử), so the title encodes a melodic phrase within the maiden's name — she is, literally, a song.
issues: Line 1: 'hái' (pick/pluck) implies gathering something — flowers or leaves for a grave mound — left unspecified rather than adding 'flowers' (Version B) since the original does not name them.
Line 8: 'thác ngàn' read as 'a thousand waterfalls' (literal) rather than 'the waterfall of a thousand years' (Version B's temporal reading); either is defensible.

Line 9: 'soi nghiêng' is tilted/slanting self-reflection in water; 'mirrored across many shores' compresses the slant and the reflection into one phrase — the exact posture of the tree-woman leaning over water is slightly condensed.
Line 2: 'jade stele' for 'bia xanh' — xanh means green/blue-green; jade is a culturally loaded but not certain reading.

en_body:
"I have tasted the heat of the stars...and oh all of them tasted so very sweet!" (Hoàng-Ân)

...I have tasted the ardent warmth of the stars — and the flavor of Light upon my body, melting sweet — that intoxicating substance of Love for this Life. I have arched upward into the high vault of sky, embracing and nourishing Existence with the blue-green that reminds us to reach always toward the principle of Truth-Goodness-Beauty! ...and oh how dizzyingly rapturous is the fierce vital force of Passion.

...this world: with sky and clouds and wind, with those ivory-jade satellites we call moon, we call stars. And Art — the sublimated Grace — is the passionate rapture of Creation, as though inheriting the first principle of the Creator when fashioning this vast and boundless world...

...Sound and Movement make the mortal world that enraptures all living beings...thoughts and feelings like a small boat adrift upon the face of the water, like a kite swollen full of wind — its body taut, soaring high...

...and today I suddenly understood, in the resplendent light of the sun, while reading Thoreau: "The sun is a daytime star!" ...taste it, won't you — that sensory knowing of this miraculous universe...drunk with the golden, flooding Joy of the star at midday, nearest of all to the tender earth that nourishes every creature: grass and flower, beast and bird, humankind — full of judgment and dispute — yet all desire Life, desire Joy, desire to partake of Life's wild and ardent heat. Life was given so that we might live this Grace most wholly and completely...

...stars and mountains and a shared cup of wine, a cup of tea...and poetry with music with paintings of resonance and color: under the vault of sky and in the light of the daytime star together with the luminaries of the night...the shimmering reflection upon a clear and wholesome spring of water...let this manifold Life be sublimated: "Suffering is sunlight transforming the body of a tear into cloud to return..." (Phạm Thiên Thư)

(23/07/2007)
note: \"Đấng hóa-sinh\" is a Sino-Vietnamese compound (化生) meaning one who generates life through transformation — closer to the Daoist Zhuangzi's \"化\" (hua) than to the Biblical Creator, though the poet likely braids both registers deliberately.
issues: "mầu xanh" is literally the color blue-green (sky-and-leaf ambiguity); "blue-green" preserves the ambiguity but sounds less natural than "the green" — either is defensible.
"đấng hóa-sinh" (the one who transforms existence into life) carries a Buddhist-Daoist resonance that "the Creator" flattens into a Christian register; no clean English equivalent.
"thị-phi" (thị = right, phi = wrong; idiom for gossip, quarrel, moral disputation) — "judgment and dispute" is closest in register but loses the Buddhist ethical flavor of the compound.

The Phạm Thiên Thư closing line — "biến thân giọt lệ làm mây trở về" — the syntax in English is still slightly compressed; "transforming the body of a tear into cloud to return" is faithful but reads as translation.

en_title: NGUYỄN THÁI HỌC and the VIỆT-NAM QUỐC-DÂN-ĐẢNG
en_body:
NGUYỄN THÁI HỌC and the
VIỆT-NAM QUỐC-DÂN-ĐẢNG

"The road is hard — not hard because rivers block or mountains divide —
 But hard because the heart dreads the mountain, fears the river."
 (Nguyễn Thái Học)


 Under the colonial regime, the French colonial government labored hard to keep the people of Vietnam imprisoned within a circle of Darkness and Ignorance. One of the measures fashioned to produce an ignorant people was the restriction of the schooling age: beyond that limit, one could no longer enter the public schools. And so Vietnamese parents would commonly falsify their children's ages, recording them younger than their years. Because of this, the precise birth date of Nguyễn Thái Học remains uncertain to this day. According to his birth certificate, he was born on the first day of the twelfth month, 1904 — yet according to legend, he belonged to the Year of Quý-Mão — that is, 1902.
 Nguyễn Thái Học's homeland lay in the village of Thổ-Tang, canton of Lương-Điền, prefecture of Vĩnh-Tường, province of Vĩnh-Yên. His father, Nguyễn Văn Hách, was a small farmer; his mother, Nguyễn Thị Quỳnh, traded in cloth. Nguyễn Thái Học was the eldest son. Nguyễn Thái Nho, his younger brother, was also a martyr — who gave his life for the Vietnamese Nation and People.

 While Nguyễn Thái Học was still a student at the Higher School of Commerce, he and his companions — Nhượng-Tống, Phạm Tuấn Lâm (Dật-Công), and Phạm Tuấn Tài (Mộng-Tiên) — founded in 1926 the publishing house Nam-Đồng Thư-Xã, hoping to raise the people's wisdom by way of Culture. At first, Nguyễn Thái Học still placed his faith in French socialism and the international parties; he had petitioned again and again to be received by the French governors-general, to lay before them his programs for reform — and was met, each time, with utter disappointment. Seeing at last that no cooperation was possible with the colonists, in the middle of 1927, Nguyễn Thái Học and his comrades resolved to enter the road of Revolution.

 The Việt-Nam Quốc-Dân-Đảng was founded with the purpose of gathering brothers, sisters, and comrades, and of employing armed force to struggle for Independence for the Nation-Republic. The structure of organization was divided into cells (chi-bộ), each group composed of nineteen persons — for under the laws then in force, any assembly of more than nineteen required prior permission from the authorities. Each cell was made up of four departments: Finance, Propaganda, Intelligence, and Administration. Each cell then elected a cell-leader and one delegate to the provincial branch (tỉnh-bộ). The provincial branch was assembled from those delegates — likewise divided into four departments, and electing a minister to serve in the higher party branch. Above this stood the regional branch (kỳ-bộ), organized on the same principles. The Central Branch (Tổng-bộ) was the supreme organ of the Party — with four additional departments: Military Affairs, Foreign Affairs, Supervision, and Assassination.
 The program of activities of the Việt-Nam Quốc-Dân-Đảng was divided into three periods: the embryonic period, operating entirely in secrecy; the preparatory period, still in secrecy; and the final period — Sabotage. But egg hurled against stone — Defeat held no surprise: what shone, magnificently, were those indomitable spirits, those exemplary mirrors of Self-Belief so high one thought they could push aside the sky itself, tilt the earth, to bring Independence to the Nation-Republic. Yet even in their momentary defeat, they still made their offering to the long struggle of a small nation — forever threatened by the violence of foreign Invasion. That indomitable spirit gave the people a patient endurance for the long-term work of protecting the People, guarding the Nation.

 On the 17th of June, 1929, Nguyễn Thái Học and 12 martyrs of the Việt-Nam Quốc-Dân-Đảng were brought before the guillotine at Yên-Báy. He was the last to mount it. He gave his life — only after crying out in full, whole and complete, those four words: "Việt-Nam Muôn Năm..!!"

 The following is the roll of the 13 martyrs of the Việt-Nam Quốc-Dân-Đảng who were slaughtered with savage barbarity: Nguyễn Thái Học, Phó Đức Chính, Bùi Tư Toàn, Nguyễn Hát Thân, Bùi Văn Chuân, Vũ Thích, Nguyễn Án, Lê Văn Tư, Nguyễn Đình Hiên, Nguyễn Ngọc Tập, Nguyễn Tư Dật, Nguyễn Văn Tín, Hà Văn Lao, Đào Văn Nhít, Đặng Hữu Hiến, Nguyễn Văn Tỵ, Nguyễn Văn Ông, Lê Văn Tự, Bùi Văn Du, Bùi Văn Tuyết, Cao Văn Chính, Mai Viết Chính, Hoàng Văn Vọng, Ngô Văn Đu, Lê Văn Khôi (Thanh Giang), Hà Cập, Nguyễn Văn Tỉnh, Nguyễn Văn Tiềm, Đỗ Văn Tư, Nguyễn Văn Kiêm, Lê Xuân Huy, Nguyễn Văn Toàn, Nguyễn Đắc Bằng, Nguyễn Văn Chu, Nguyễn Như Thông, Nguyễn Như Liên (Ngọc-Tĩnh), Vũ Xuân Kiểm, Trần Đức Tài, Hoàng Công Tiển, Bùi Xuân Mai, Ngô Thanh Thuyết, Ngô Hải Hoàng, Nguyễn Văn Thinh, Nguyễn Đức Liên.

(10/12/2012)
note: The epigraph is attributed to Nguyễn Thái Học himself — a couplet that circulated widely in nationalist circles; 'ngại' and 'e' are near-synonyms both meaning to dread/be wary of, with 'e' slightly softer, rendered here as 'dreads' and 'fears' to preserve the tonal difference.
issues: Epigraph word order: Vietnamese has 'ngại núi, e sông' (dreads mountain, fears river) — Version A reversed these; final corrects to dread-mountain-first, fear-river-second.
'Dân-Quốc' is a compound meaning both People and Nation/Republic; rendered as 'Nation-Republic' and 'Nation and People' in different instances — no single English word captures the compound.

'tổng Lương-Điền' translated as 'canton' — this is the closest Western administrative equivalent but not a standard English rendering in Vietnamese historical texts; some prefer to leave 'tổng' untranslated.

The roll header says '13 martyrs' but the list contains many more names — this matches the Vietnamese original and reflects a historical distinction (13 executed at Yên-Báy specifically; the roll extends to all VNQDĐ martyrs). Preserved as-is.

'tấm gương bất-khuất' literally means 'indomitable mirrors' (tấm gương = mirror, also model/exemplar) — rendered as 'exemplary mirrors of Self-Belief' to hold both senses; 'mirrors' may puzzle English readers unfamiliar with this Vietnamese idiom.

en_body:
Longing — how lightly it rises, how it stirs Fragrance—
I tie a bow of verse with a thread of smoke,
Wrap cold-pinched Love into the breath,
Burn the love poem with the sound of silk strings.

A sneeze — a quiet joy — I watch for someone coming:
Is there anyone, someone out there, who also longs?
A breath loosening gently, draws itself to Dream—
White Thoughts wandering...a rosy Dawn.

(23/05/2007)
note: The title 'Nhớ' carries both 'to miss someone' and 'to remember' simultaneously — the poem enacts that double meaning: the speaker sneezes (folk belief: someone is thinking of you) and wonders if the longed-for person is also longing back.
issues: Line 1: 'rises' is interpretive — the Vietnamese has only 'how light' (nhẹ quá) + 'stirs fragrance' (gây hương); the upward movement is added for poetic continuity with the smoke imagery in line 2, not sourced from the text.
Line 6: 'nhớ' echoes the title exactly — 'longs' preserves that thematic echo; 'remembers' is equally valid but loses the recursion.

Line 3: 'se-sắt' (literally cold-nipped, iron-tight) rendered as 'cold-pinched' — accurate but an unusual English compound; no better single-word option preserves both the temperature and constriction.

en_title: Through GLORIA in D, R. 589—Antonio Vivaldi
en_body:
Let rise the raging tempest of Fire—
Burn my Soul once and once more again,
But within so many dreams
My Soul tastes the sweetness of Fire,
Higher still the tongue of flame licks up to Heaven—
Burning to ash all palaces and towers of illusion—
I would shatter everything that is not Real,
For before Truth I die standing a thousand times..!!

But from the distance a sweet cool stream of music drifts,
Resounding so gently, it carries Grace to me,
Oh, the dream again — wet — pierces my Soul..!!
"...et in terra pax hominibus bonae voluntatis..."*

Grief and sorrow, so gently, ripen into a swoon in the cradle—
Let me lie down and spread wide my arms and legs,
Hearing Fire — somewhere — seeping into the earth..!!


* "...and on earth peace, blessedness for mankind..."
** GLORIA in D, R. 589, Vivaldi Antonio
   Academic Russian Chorus,
   Conductor: Alexander Svedunikov.

(12/05/2012)
note: The title preposition 'Nhờ' means 'by means of / through / thanks to' — the poem is written under the spell of the Vivaldi, not merely about it; 'Through' captures this dependence faithfully.
issues: Line 11: 'ướt đâm' (wet-pierces) is ambiguous — whether 'wet' modifies the dream itself or the act of piercing; 'the dream again — wet — pierces my Soul' keeps the word but the syntax remains uncertain
Line 6: 'điện/đài' uses a slash in the Vietnamese, suggesting the poet held both 'palace' and 'tower' in mind; 'palaces and towers' honors this but adds plurality not explicit in the original

Footnote: the Vietnamese gloss of 'bonae voluntatis' uses 'hạnh-phúc' (blessedness/happiness), not the KJV 'goodwill toward men'; 'blessedness for mankind' follows the poet's own reading over the liturgical standard

en_body:
The Naïve-Ardent Fish
23/12/2005

Mr. Stone, Dear Friend Phạm Hoàng-Thành, Most Beloved...
 I have flung open the cage of my chest to receive the mingled, teeming sounds that Life
 bestows: Night and Day.
 I lie down upon the grass to hear the crickets calling one to another and to feel Time settling
 into stillness in the fleeting instant of interweaving: Heaven and Earth.
 That is I — the contemporary painter.
 Quite a long road I have wandered and groped to seek for my soul those paintings that satisfy —
 those you shall see on this website.
 No ambition to carry the whole world to you. But in confidence
 I send my greeting from Me...a greeting of many colors.

Those are the succinct words in the lines of "Tự Bạch" [Self-Disclosure] on Hoàng-Thành's website! Please allow me, dear Friend, to call you by that name of utmost Meaning — for Nguyệt alone! It reminds Nguyệt of the ancestral lands, the homeland of the family lineage that Nguyệt has never laid eyes upon. And please accept warmest greetings, dear Friend — for it was through reading your biography that Nguyệt came to know that her rare and precious Soul-Companion of the Heart had journeyed from Hà-Nội, the imperial capital Nguyệt has known only through poetry, prose, and the tender longing melodies of the beloved artist-children of the North who have departed that cherished ancient capital so far behind! You are Nguyệt's only friend from the North: this too is a singularity belonging to Nguyệt alone!

TENSION: this series of abstract paintings is supremely outstanding! To Nguyệt it carries something of Andrew Wyeth in its lines — sharp and delicate. The extreme contrasting colors between black and white with very few warm-spectrum hues like red and orange-red alongside the cool spectrum of blue: reveal to Nguyệt the succinct, contained depth and richness of Hoàng-Thành's soul. Nguyệt feels that through those paintings, they are Symbols of the vision Hoàng-Thành holds of Life at large! Though dear Friend bears witness to the low valleys of human existence full of entanglement, altogether Hoàng-Thành is very optimistic, very confident in the self-direction of the individual within society: in active engagement and the striving to rise above! Nguyệt very much loves the composition of the solid spaces in the paintings against the vast background ground — especially the white ground! And Nguyệt is particularly enchanted by the way Hoàng-Thành dots and dashes those warm-toned strokes, sparse yet luminous — like positive Will and the note of joy within the extremely vigorous and powerful character of the painter!

CHÂN-DUNG: what can Nguyệt say about this Series! Nguyệt can feel the powerful character of a Man through this series from Hoàng-Thành! Intermingled and harmonious are the gentle romance of the poetic and musical quality of contemporary Vietnamese art, and also the utterly masculine, sexual artistry of the painter! Nguyệt is particularly moved by the word "TÔI" — supremely simple yet bearing within it a person rooted to the beloved homeland of us both, within the painter far from home, Phạm Hoàng-Thành. Nguyệt knows Hoàng-Thành's ambitions are very encompassing: dear Friend knows well his own gifted nature and has plans to stand alongside the great luminaries of international fine arts; yet in the first Figurative Series that dear Friend is about to exhibit — a series of self-revelation: CHÂN-DUNG: dear Friend has chosen the chemise shirt, and upon each of those self-bearing shirts there is inscribed in the simplest way one cherished word of the national script: "TÔI"... Gratitude to this gifted contemporary painter: for in any case, being at once a dear friend of Hoàng-Thành's and a child of a country that, though heroic, has borne so many injustices in history — Nguyệt too holds a small measure of pride and honor in knowing a painter of such great talent, possessed of a character supremely deep yet truly endearing, humble, and — in his vast and deep knowledge of the immense, splendid, resplendent world of international fine arts — Phạm Hoàng-Thành still remembers himself as a simple child of the complex contemporary Vietnamese art world.

PHÁC-HỌA: Nguyệt is enormously enchanted by this series of Hoàng-Thành's in Indian ink and graphite on white paper! Very strange! Very sexual through the "Apples and Fish," "Woman and Fish" — very sensuous with "Nude Woman," "Woman with Coiled Hair" — very bold and vigorous through the self-portrait sketches of the painter — very exotic with the supremely classical drawn strokes of "Woman Smoking," "Male Model" — yet supremely ethereal and revealing the innocence and gentle goodness through the sketches of Laurence!

Please accept my congratulations, dear Soul-Companion of Nguyệt's Heart! Nguyệt is deeply honored that Hoàng-Thành cherishes and trusts her enough to share with Nguyệt this creation — this life's work of such depth and such magnificent stature! The Website itself has already proved the artistry and the intensity of everything Hoàng-Thành has devoted to a lifetime's passion for art! All that remains is to await Time for the day when Hoàng-Thành's work is repaid as it deserves! Time is the unit of Measure for the Will of ardor: and the intensity of ardor is the concentration of Will! Awaiting the glad tidings from Hoàng-Thành announcing the exhibition of Your First Figurative Series: CHÂN-DUNG!
note: \"Tự Bạch\" (Self-Disclosure/Confession) is the name of Hoàng-Thành's artist statement on his website — Version A mistranslated it as \"Self-Portrait\" (which would be \"Tự Họa\" or \"Chân Dung\"); corrected to \"Self-Disclosure\" following Version B.
issues: "chấm-phá" — a specific ink-wash technique (dot-and-splash/dash); rendered as "dots and dashes" which is functional but loses the painterly weight of the term
"cường-mảnh" — simultaneously vigorous AND lean/slender in physique; no English equivalent captures both qualities; "vigorous and powerful" drops the slenderness
"áo tự-thân" — the shirts as self-bearing objects inscribed with the word I/TÔI; "self-bearing shirts" is unusual but the most faithful rendering available
"tự vận-hành của cá-nhân" — the self-directed movement/self-propulsion of the individual; rendered as "self-direction" which is adequate but flattens the philosophical charge

"xúc-tích" modifying the soul in TENSION paragraph — means concise/pithy, describing the economy of Hoàng-Thành's inner life; keeping A's "succinct" here but the pairing with "depth" is slightly awkward

en_body:
The arms of Winter —
lean and bare, pressed against the ice-blue sky —
Wind ferries the dried brown leaves
still clinging to the branch.
I sit amid the mortal world
singing the farewell song...

If anyone returns,
go visit for me that first blossom of the season
blooming in the midst of Departure —
If anyone returns, recall for me through a breath
the fragrance of old days, sweet upon soft hair...

The sunlight yearns to lift the blue sky
and loose the clouds to drift without destination —
Wind turns upon itself, curling the red dust to flight;
The worn path that someone once walked through
lies indifferent, careless — alongside Memory.

My Grief ripens in the open air —
I sit dazed, singing old songs of days gone by;
Someone catches at my collar, I chant the ballad of years and months —
That one walks away,
without turning their head to look back —
Alone, I —
I long, alone...

(13/01/2013)
note: \"nhân-gian\" (mortal world / human realm) carries a Buddhist register — the realm of impermanence between heaven and nirvana — which \"mortal world\" preserves better than the flatter \"human world.\"
issues: "tròn cuộn bụi đỏ bay" — the roundness/coiling of the wind movement (tròn cuộn = round-rolling) is only partially caught by "curling"; no single English phrase fully renders the circular kinetics.
"nhìn vọng" — literally to turn and look longingly into the distance; both versions render it as simply "look back," losing the aching, far-focused quality of vọng.

"hong chín" — the specific image is grief hung out in the air to dry until it ripens, like drying fruit; "ripens in the open air" is close but softens the intentional, almost domestic patience of the act.

en_body:
The crystal spheres waltz
to the music of Imagination — written in
the formulas of Logic and Mathematics.
The rhythmic sound of the invisible Music
transmitted through the trembling layers of waves —
The Meaning of that uttermost Music
can only be understood by the Heart through the settling
of the intellect.
When the crystal spheres waltz
they must harmonize into One with the melody —
Within those crystal spheres are held
pearl-Thoughts and crystal-Emotions
And the trembling of thought within —
How to sink and dissolve into the cosmic melody
that governs the spinning of the crystal spheres
so that together they may follow the orbital waltz,
along the revolution that has surpassed even the deep resonance
of Silence...
(University Of Houston, Central Campus,
 Houston, TX, USA: Spring 1981)
note: Quỹ đạo (orbit/orbital path) doubles as the governing metaphor: the spheres do not merely dance but trace a determined celestial path, which is why 'orbital waltz' in line 16 must be kept — it fuses both senses of luân-vũ (revolve + waltz).
issues: trí-tuệ rendered as 'intellect' — the compound carries Buddhist prajna overtones closer to 'wisdom,' but 'intellect' fits the poem's rationalist register (Logic, Mathematics) and avoids conflating it with tâm/heart
tuyệt-cùng on line 6: grammatically modifies nhạc (Music), so 'uttermost Music' is the safer reading, but A's 'at its uttermost' treats it as predicate of the Meaning — both are defensible

âm-thanh thâm-trầm: 'deep resonance' adds a sustained sonic quality; 'thâm-trầm' means profound/subdued/deep — 'resonance' is an interpretive leap, but 'deep sounds' (A) is flat; this line remains the hardest call
Xuân 1981 rendered as 'Spring 1981' per B — the poet uses English in her own datelines so this is preferred over keeping the Vietnamese

en_body:
the golden ring-neck's beak pecks and holds a fragrant bloom
young birds skip and frolic, beating their wings, learning to fly
the green meadow overwhelmed — flooded with the colors of fresh blooms
blazing gold threaded through deep rose, mingled with charcoal violet
sunlight drunk on ferment, wind intoxicated — cloud-wings swoon along the sky-road
Spring seeps into the skin: the season of Resurrection

(4:36 p.m., Chủ-Nhật, 15/04/07)
note: Chủ-Nhật (Sunday) and 'mùa phục-sinh' (Resurrection Season / Easter) place this poem on Easter Sunday, April 15, 2007 — the seasonal and liturgical convergence is load-bearing to the poem's meaning.
issues: Line 5: 'ngây men' literally = drunk on yeast/ferment — kept as 'drunk on ferment' for fidelity, but it reads unusually in English; 'drunk on sweetness' (B) is smoother but loses the fermentation image.

Line 6: 'mùa phục-sinh' is structurally ambiguous — appositive labeling the season ('the season of Resurrection') vs. genitive ('of the Resurrection Season'); colon construction chosen as the most natural reading.
Line 1: 'rỉa' = to peck/nibble at (flower), not to preen feathers — 'pecks and holds' (A) chosen over 'preens and holds' (B) as more faithful to the bird-flower interaction.
Line 4: 'tím than' = charcoal violet specifically (than = charcoal), not merely dark violet — A's 'charcoal violet' is correct and retained.

en_title: Birthing Me, Father Cast Down the Dragon-Brush
en_body:
Born, I wept myself senseless —
Birthing me, Mother cried out — the sky shook, the clouds trembled;
Birthing me, Father cut off the dream of his days —
I was born into a place overflowing with Sacrifice.

I was born into the spinning of the earth —
The earth carries me along inside its turning —
To enter the great Round — that Cycle of all returning —
Unchanged by the presence-or-absence of me —

But: Father's Toil, Mother's Devotion, Teacher's Grace —
Each grain of rice, letters and meaning, the red blood of Ancestors —
Birthing me, Father cast down his Dragon-Brush,
So that I, in days to come — Children, Grandchildren — may repay.

(Season of Giỗ Tổ Hùng-Vương, 2005 Western Calendar.)
note: Giỗ Tổ Hùng-Vương is the annual death-anniversary commemoration of the Hùng Kings, legendary founders of the Vietnamese nation — a festival of ancestral debt structurally mirroring the poem's theme of repayment across generations.
issues: Line 7: 'cuộc-chung-quy' is philosophically dense — literally 'the common-final-cycle/round'; 'Cycle of all returning' is interpretive toward samsara/Buddhist register, which may be correct but is not provable from the text alone.
Line 8: double em-dashes across lines 7–8 create slight visual clutter, but the enjambment is faithful to the original's suspended syntax.
'Nghĩa Mẹ' rendered as 'Mother's Devotion' — 'Duty' and 'Love' are equally valid; Devotion chosen for its warmth and fit with her register.

en_title: Sometimes I Don't Know How To Feel
en_body:
And each day passes — in its rains and its sunshine...Listen: in
 one moment of Stillness is wholly contained all the Thousand Autumns.
VerdantSpring-Mùa Cầm Xanh, 7:33 p.m. cst-usa, Thứ Sáu, 10/11/2006

...The rain is falling and lightning flickers and blazes without cease along the horizon; the lightning parts the clouds and thunder rolls in deep rumbling: a miracle of the human world tonight — and among the crowd, I alone am given to feel it! Oh, God is speaking with me — the lowly one who strains to call upon Him with the whole of my existential being: begging repentance, begging to be received into the Kingdom of God — for only the humble souls are permitted by God to witness His Kingdom while the soul still carries the body as the vessel through which the Gospel is received!

...I dream of Jesus kneeling before God the Father alongside His companions — the Buddhas and the Bodhisattvas: where the Kingdom of God is revealed as the Great Vow of Compassion-Wisdom-Courage to Deliver the World: releasing all passions and attachments, offering the whole of one's existential being to save the world: Oh, I am so small, so soft and yielding before that Great Vow of Loving-Kindness — like a silkworm cocoon that has been boiled to yield its silk. Fine silk weaves a pale cloth the color of fasting: like the downy color of the small sparrow of my longing! And the deep-worn blue of the plain monk's robe. Father! I am naturally drunk on the fragrance of the Way! I naturally love the dream of Peaceful Dwelling: but the realm of my consciousness storms — a dark sea raging in the dust of Ignorance: still the body like sea-kelp is battered and tossed, erupting in aching songs that pour free from drifting in Greed, Hatred, Delusion, and the seven emotions and six desires! Still am I the instrument the Father gives to the World — the World hollows and chisels the spiritual realm into the sound of the heart-lute: Oh, those silk strings moaning the fate of the Lover! The Sea of Love rises and falls, its waves spreading grief like strings: ten strings draw blood from ten fingertips: the many Vương Thúy Kiều deep in contemplation within the instinctual consciousness! From the root — incarnating to flow downstream along the torrent, carried back to the source and returning into the same root. The night of consciousness is too long; each kṣaṇa without boundary, layer upon layer besieging: time has slipped away from the four seasons — and I stand there motionless as the karma of the ego-self, to hear how powerless I am before the realm of Non-Self, while the narrow intellect within the framework of humanity's instinctual logic still encircles me!

 "Why am I human?" and "Sometimes I don't know how to feel" (TR-i)
note: \"Vương Thúy Kiều\" is the tragic heroine of Nguyễn Du's 18th-century verse novel \"Truyện Kiều\" — the supreme symbol in Vietnamese literature of a woman of exceptional inner gifts broken by the cruelties of fate; invoking her in the plural here multiplies that archetype across all the poet's interior selves.
issues: "đa-tư" (in "những Vương Thúy Kiều đa-tư") literally means much-pondering/many-thoughts; rendered here as "deep in contemplation" but "many sorrows" (fitting Kiều's character) is also defensible.

"bập-bùng loé lên liên-tục" — "bập-bùng" suggests an intermittent flickering quality while "loé lên" is the blazing flash; the combined "flickers and blazes" attempts to hold both but a single verb cannot fully capture the compound.

"mầu lông tơ con se-sẻ nhỏ" — the comparison is specifically to the color of the sparrow's down feathers ("mầu lông tơ" = color of down-feathers); "downy color" softens this slightly but avoids an awkward literal rendering.

"thiên-thu" rendered as "Thousand Autumns" (faithful to the classical Sino-Vietnamese compound) rather than the more familiar "Eternity" — readers unfamiliar with the idiom may find it opaque, but the literal image is the poet's own.

en_title: To Live One Season, to Love a Lifetime
en_body:
(For LuiKang (Nguoi Chet) of his creation: Nhac Death)
......................................................................................


So hesitant, every step searching homeward...I feel my way on bare heels through the labyrinth, longing for Your hand to reach out and lead me through. The naïve child within me stirs again with an old yearning: a hero, from long ago, to seek and lean upon...

Suddenly night descends, and You manifest within me:

A symbol that shelters the gentle Dream of Happiness. The Hero within me is everything within You: The full road of life You have walked, Your soles worn and roughened; You pause after those things You have chosen as Your own: It is the room You keep in order, tending to yourself. It is the stillness of a space where You may wrestle — turning thoughts, choosing a way of seeing. It is the scarf You fold with careful hands and lift about Your neck. It is the shirt, the pressed crease of trousers, the bearing You choose to wear. It is the hair You smooth with patience, combing each strand. It is the humble ceremony of tying Your sash...It is the life-and-death Passion — Romantic — upon each sharp and flat note of the strings. It is the open stride. It is the straight and truthful word..!!

My Hero has chosen a vantage point:

A measure of living — serene and distilled...Your ordinary Dream is what I dream grandly, after so many years of struggling through the experience of life, having come face to face with so many kinds of men: The upper classes and the intellectuals; the misery and humiliation of those mired in ruin; the ambitions, the feverish jostlings; the drifting loose to satisfy lifelong hungers; the selfishness born of singular instinct; the seeking — with or without thought behind it; the antagonism born of political perspective; the hasty trust in supernatural doctrine; the closedness born merely of suspicion; the frenzy from the ache of love-longing; even the sacrifices made for Ideal...

You have passed through so many contemplations, so many searchings!
You still wrestle with so much You wish to say!
Night comes to You as it comes to me — to all of humanity —
My Night reaches out to find Your Night!
In the Darkness, tender and miraculous:
Let Your hand reach out and find and hold my hand!
There are times I lie weeping, fainting as I call Your name:
In sobs — oh, too full, too real:

The spiritual hell has its own angel: When Love returns in the storm of remembrance, You fill me like the velvety Darkness of a cradle — Suddenly awakened, remembering that Your Heart too is soft, warm with blood, that there are nights You too weep inside: Remember me — who remembers You and lies weeping, in the sharp and stabbing pain of longing — that You too ache...

...and guide me, carry me into Your heart through this tangle of grief: grant me, even so, today — however fleeting! To live one season, we shall love a lifetime...whatever sweetness there be, it is eternity's keeping..!!

(29/01/2013)
note: \"Thiên-thâu\" is a Sino-Vietnamese literary term (千秋, qiānqiū) meaning \"a thousand autumns\" — a classical figure for eternity; the rendering as \"eternity's keeping\" preserves the sense of permanence without literalizing the numeral.
issues: "cơn nhớ" — "storm of remembrance" (A) vs "surge of memory" (B); Vietnamese cơn suggests a fit or sudden onset; "storm" is more evocative but "surge" may be more precise
"thiên-thâu" is archaic Vietnamese for time-without-end; "eternity's keeping" is an interpretive gloss — no single English word is exact
"dọ-dẫm gót trần" — rendered as "bare heels" (gót = heel, trần = bare/naked) rather than both versions' looser "bare-footed"; this is more faithful but slightly unusual in English idiom
"nương dõi" — the compound means to lean upon and closely follow; "lean upon" is slightly softened to "lean upon" in the final; some nuance of devoted tracking is lost

en_body:
INTIME EXPLOSION.......................................................................
............Gisèle Prassinos.............................................................

Intime explosion dans la tere
Soupirs du crocus
qui s'interroge coquettement:
jaune ou blanc?


THE HEAVEN-SUBLIME EXPLOSION...........................................................
............Hoàng-Ân, after the spirit..............................................................

The Explosion — utter-complete, to the uttermost depths — within the earth's heart
The frangipani blossoms sigh—
In Rapture they preen, they pose — and pretend to ask themselves:
"Yellow or white?"


(Shreveport, LA: Thursday, 12/07/2012)
note: \"Hoàng-Ân dịch ý\" means she is translating Prassinos's meaning/spirit rather than her words; \"after the spirit\" echoes the fine-arts convention (\"after Rembrandt\") and fits her own English register better than \"spirit-rendering.\"
issues: "Thiên-Tuyệt" is a poetic hapax compound (thiên = heaven/sky + tuyệt = sublime/absolute); "Heaven-Sublime" approximates but cannot fully convey the sense of absolute transcendence fused with celestial register — neither "Celestial" nor "Heaven" alone closes the gap.

"làm dáng" carries a specifically feminine, coquettish vanity (the crocus's coquettement in the source); "they preen, they pose" catches the doubling but softens the self-regarding flirtatiousness slightly.

en_body:
...You, oh You..!!
...since loving You, I have slowly found my way into the vast World of the human realm...and then sensed, drifting wide through it, the luminous Presence — the silent Being of the Life-Giver...and I was returned to a wholesome realm, sweet as the fairy tales in the hearts of good and gentle children...and learned to love within my own limitations...

...God loves, and all living souls arise from the karmic causes of that Love; so when You stirred Love in me: through the feeling of loving You, I experienced Love and came to understand the love within God's heart...Love parted the current of my existence like water splitting at its source, flowing wide and boundless: suddenly finding, in the fury of passion, that Truth is to live wholly with desires most real: God is ever silent — yet like the lesson written in the natural phenomena of the Five Elements — Earth, Water, Wood, Fire, Metal: we return to the Life-Giver the knowledge that created us by living wholly within the intoxicating passions of our human essence; as this great wide world also holds within it wind and storm and volcano and the sweeping vortex of meteorites through a universe of layered heat, burning endlessly into stars in their ceaseless becoming...

...and oh, You: it is You who pumped the living sap to nourish the happiness-vine within me, until it blossomed into words of praise for Your very existence...thank You for sheltering the flowering season of Love — so rich in color and fragrance — in my life that stretches upward to receive all the Grace and tenderness of Your affection..!!

(7:00 p.m. CST-USA, Tuesday, 28/08/07)
note: "Đấng hóa-sinh" is the poet's syncretic God-name — literally "the One who transforms into life/gives birth" — rendered "Life-Giver" to hold both the Buddhist becoming and Christian creation resonance she braids throughout her work.
issues: "Ấy" is a Vietnamese pronoun of intimate third-person distance ("that one") — capitalizing "You" preserves the devotional register but loses the slight pronoun-as-mystery quality the poet uses throughout.
"dung-dưỡng" (stanza 3) carries both "to shelter" and "to nourish" simultaneously; choosing one word flattens the dual sense.
"rướn nhận" = to strain/stretch upward to receive — both versions render this adequately but the physical exertion in the verb ("rướn" implies craning/reaching with effort) is softened in English.

"ngũ-hành" lists gỗ (Wood) not "timber" — both versions correctly use Wood, but the cosmological order Earth-Water-Wood-Fire-Metal differs from the more familiar Chinese sequence; preserved as-is per the Vietnamese.

en_body:
Love — salt-sweet, the savor of sugar,
a bundle of sticky rice with corn and beans, the porch, the gate aslant...
Love — blood-salty, the heart of the lotus,
hot tears streaming, pink wax drops falling,
Love — ocean-swells, the haughty island,
desolate amid crystal wings on all four sides
Love — oh, how to say it fully:
Love of homeland, love of country, love of people, love of you..!!
In a foreign land, lights illumine the shimmering canals
Fields of Tulips like a river of blossoms
The bright sea, draped in rainbow-colored cloth..
Alone, I strain toward your shadow, windswept,
amid the furious stream of the multitude..
A sliver of the fatherland in the far-away land..
Love: oh — weightier than grace and all obligation
Husband and wife, father and mother: what could surpass this!?

(24/11/2005)
note: The poem was written November 2005 while the poet was in the Netherlands (canals, tulip fields kept as loanwords even in the Vietnamese original) — the foreign-land stanza is geographically grounded, not purely imagined.
issues: Line 11 — 'dù vải mầu cầu vồng': 'dù' is ambiguous (canopy/umbrella noun vs. concessive conjunction 'though/even if'); 'draped in rainbow-colored cloth' assumes the noun reading; the conjunction reading would give 'The bright sea, though only rainbow-colored cloth..' — a more bittersweet exile image.

Line 12 — 'chải-bương': rendered as 'windswept' (B's reading), which captures the disheveled-by-wind sense; the compound also carries a connotation of struggling forward under duress that 'windswept' alone doesn't fully convey.

Line 15 — 'ơn nghĩa': the Vietnamese compound holds both spiritual beneficence (ơn = grace/gift) and moral duty (nghĩa = obligation/righteousness) in a single dense ethical weight; 'grace and all obligation' splits them but no single English word captures the compound.

Line 3 — 'tim sen' (lotus heart/core): the seed-core of the lotus is distinctively bitter in Vietnamese culinary/spiritual register, amplifying the salt-blood image; 'the heart of the lotus' is accurate but loses the bitter undertone.

en_body:
...a pot of tea, green grass held in the mouth: breathing the smoke in and exhaling the breath out, a heap of poems snatching at rhyme-sounds...the amber cup rings its amber note, rounds the drum-beat; the lute crackles and flings its light, bursts into string-song: tích tịch tình tang, arms embracing the book...

...golden as the cup, wringing clear light: drawing circles round the smoke's spiraling circles...sitting in Silence, thinking of the dreamed one — submerged, hidden — practicing the making of poems, coaxing the tones from the lute — he tilts his face, shaping, searching for himself...turning and turning to spin the silk of Dreams. chewing a blade of grass to offer to the beak of the golden-ringed warbler at the cloud's rim: wandering-singing, greeting the gathered clouds, beckoning the sun to kiss the line of the horizon — shielding the eyes with caresses, dreaming into the Future...

...trees full of leaves, fish full in the pond: beneath the banyan's canopy visiting the ripened seeds...flying here and there in search of his full name, to carve it at the heart's center — pressing a love-seal into tree bark to remember the Love...alighting on wind-feet, joining wings with phượng and loan, circling, circling, then returning to gather...

...lying beneath the grass with eyes flung wide: taking all the surrounding scene into two filming lenses — beneath the courtyard sky, a brow not yet lined by wrinkles of quarreling...looking up to a sky brilliant with shining stars...someone tilts the shared earth until the water thickens, humming and humming — like the hollow of a conch bearing the Ocean's longings in every hue of Myth...inhaling the fragrant smoke, the vast Past suddenly blurs the eyes with tears...

...why so bound by Convention
 yet
 still
 cannot
 bear
...to come and seek each other?...

(10/03/2007)
note: Phượng and loan are paired mythological birds (phoenix and luan-bird) symbolizing lovers in classical Vietnamese/Chinese literary tradition; keeping them untranslated preserves the allusion that both translators correctly left intact.
issues: "làm sánh nước vang ù ù" — sánh means viscous/thickening, not trembling; "thickens, humming and humming" is the most faithful rendering but the synesthetic image resists clean English

"chim khuyên vàng ngấn vân" — chim khuyên is a white-eye (Zosterops), not strictly a warbler; "golden-ringed warbler at the cloud's rim" preserves the collar-ring and cloud-edge (ngấn vân) but the bird species is approximate

"dưới sân trán chưa vết nhăn cãi-vã" — dưới sân (beneath the courtyard/yard) and the brow-clause are syntactically compressed in the Vietnamese; placement of "beneath the courtyard sky" as a separate phrase is an editorial bridge

"chắn mắt ve-vuốt tưởng vào tương-lai" — chắn mắt (shielding/blocking the eyes) and ve-vuốt (caressing) run together as one gesture; "shielding the eyes with caresses" fuses them but loses the visual of hand-over-brow against the horizon

en_body:
The hardships of Heaven-and-Earth, wind and dust—
Have cast me into exile and torment, O dear one!
I kneel and bow in the midst of the Void—
Whether one stirs it murky, whether one fills it full—
Grant me this one blessed portion,
Grant me one full sleep, dreamless and whole,
Grant me the sight of green leaves
And the small butterfly drifting around where I sit,
Grant me to hear the sound of laughter,
To look out upon Life and see only children.

I kneel, I bow before demons and spirits both—
Whether bitter, whether venomous, whether it be Justice—
Then hold this body here, I beg you—
While I alone soar high as a kite in spring,
While I alone bathe in Poetry,
While I alone, drunk, sing the songs of Love—
The hardships of Heaven-and-Earth, wind and dust—
I sit and sing the triumphant song of Joy.

(30/05/2012)
note: The lục bát meter (alternating 6- and 8-syllable lines, interlocking rhyme) gives the Vietnamese a chant-like, circular quality — the closing refrain ('Gian-nan thiên-địa phong-trần') returning to the opening is a formal device of the form, not mere repetition, and signals defiant acceptance rather than despair.
issues: Line 4: 'Whether one stirs it murky' is slightly awkward — 'khuấy đục' (to stir until muddy) has no clean English equivalent that keeps the parallel structure intact.
Line 2: 'O dear one!' renders 'Người ơi' — the addressee is deliberately ambiguous in Vietnamese (God, the universe, a beloved), and any English choice slightly collapses that openness.
Line 18: 'hoan-ca khải-hoàn' is a doubled triumph-word (jubilant-song + victory-return); 'triumphant song of Joy' preserves the sense but loses the musical doubling of the original.

en_body:
I Thank You
(28/01/2013)

A branch of flowers, languid upon the piano keys—
The bloom has burst its petals open, sharp thorns violet-blue.
You have gone home, yet locked the wind inside—
Wind, soft...orchid fragrance...
Wind, drifting...fragrance dissolving...

You left, forgetting to take the Heaven and Earth with you—
the Years and Months echo through the Pure Hall,
Heaven and Earth grown dizzy with Melody.
And so I sit at the foot of the clouds,
my Heart painting a Rainbow—
Clouds in peach-pink and purple—
Velvet-Longing beyond all measure lifts itself onto the strings,

The tone of the strings...the color blue...
The key of the strings...the color azure...
note: The poem's title echoes a formal Vietnamese expression of gratitude with the polite particle 'xin,' giving it a tone of reverence rather than simple thanks — the beloved is being addressed almost as one thanks a departing sacred presence.
issues: Title: 'Xin Cảm Ơn Anh' carries a humble/supplicatory particle ('xin') that 'I Thank You' softens but cannot fully render — 'I Humbly Thank You' is more literal but less musical.

'thanh-cung' (Sino-Vietnamese compound): rendered as 'Pure Hall' (a sacred/celestial space); could carry a musical nuance ('Hall of Pure Sound') — context supports both but the poem's register favors the sacred reading.
'mầu lơ' rendered as 'azure' — 'lơ' denotes a pale, washed-out blue closer to sky-blue or pale azure; 'azure' is the best English poetic equivalent but slightly richer than the original.
'nhung-nhớ' = Velvet-Longing is correct (nhung = velvet, nhớ = longing/missing) and Version B rightly preserves this compound — confirmed faithful.
